title: Arch: Scheduler does not verify Adjutant is alive before sending heartbeat
description: The scheduler's sendHeartbeat() blindly sends tmux send-keys to the Adjutant session. If the session has died (Claude Code crashed, tmux session killed), the heartbeat silently fails and the user gets no status reports until someone notices. This is the Phase 3 health check (adj-051.3), but it's architecturally concerning that Phase 1+2 shipped without it — the entire value proposition of 'reducing babysitting' depends on reliable heartbeat delivery. The scheduler should at minimum log a warning when the heartbeat fails that's visible in the dashboard, or ideally call isAdjutantAlive() before sending. Note: adj-051.3.1 and adj-051.3.2 already cover this, but they're marked as separate phase. Consider promoting adj-051.3 to P1 since without it, the system is unreliable.
close_reason: Superseded by adj-051.5 restructure — scheduler.ts deleted

title: Bug: Agent instructions have no context window management strategy
description:
File: .claude/agents/adjutant.md

The agent instructions define a heartbeat procedure that calls 5+ MCP tools per cycle (list_agents, list_beads x2, send_message per stale agent, send_message to user). Each heartbeat response from the agent + tool results adds ~500-2000 tokens to the conversation context.

With hourly heartbeats, after 24 hours the conversation will have ~12,000-48,000 tokens of accumulated heartbeat data. After a few days, the agent will hit its context window limit and either:
1. Start dropping early conversation context (losing startup instructions)
2. Error out with a context length exceeded error
3. Produce increasingly degraded responses as important context is pushed out

There is no mechanism to:
- Compact or summarize previous heartbeat results
- Start a fresh conversation while preserving agent identity
- Limit the number of heartbeats before a context reset

Suggested fix:
Add a 'Context Management' section to the agent instructions:
- After N heartbeats (e.g., 12), the scheduler should respawn the agent with a fresh context
- Or: instruct the agent to use /compact or similar context management commands
- Or: design the heartbeat to be stateless (no reference to previous heartbeats needed)
close_reason: By design: heartbeats are stateless (fresh MCP tool calls each cycle), Claude Code has built-in auto-compaction, and SessionStart hooks re-inject PRIME.md after compaction

title: Arch: No heartbeat-on-startup — user waits up to 1 hour for first report
description: The scheduler starts an hourly cron job (0 * * * *) but does not fire an immediate heartbeat on startup. If the backend starts at 10:15, the first heartbeat won't fire until 11:00 — a 45-minute gap where the user has no Adjutant status report. For the stated goal of 'reducing user babysitting,' an immediate first heartbeat after spawn is essential. Fix: call sendHeartbeat() once immediately after startScheduler(), with a short delay (e.g., 30 seconds) to allow the Adjutant agent to finish initializing. Or add a startup delay + initial fire option to the scheduler.

title: Bug: sendHeartbeat injects multi-line text that may exceed tmux paste buffer limit
description:
File: backend/src/services/scheduler.ts, lines 70-94

The heartbeat prompt is a 13-line, ~700+ character string sent via a single tmux send-keys -l command. While tmux send-keys -l handles literal text correctly, very long strings can exceed the tmux paste buffer or input buffer limits depending on terminal configuration.

More critically, the multi-line text is sent as a single send-keys invocation. Claude Code's input handler receives all this text at once as stdin. If the Claude Code process is mid-response (e.g., still processing a previous heartbeat or user message), the injected text will appear as garbled input interleaved with any ongoing terminal output.

There is no guard to check whether the agent is currently idle/waiting-for-input before injecting the heartbeat prompt. If the agent is mid-turn processing a previous heartbeat (which could take minutes if many agents/beads exist), the new heartbeat text will be queued in the terminal's input buffer and delivered when the agent finishes — potentially causing double-processing.

Suggested fix:
1. Before sending the heartbeat, check if the agent is idle (via isAdjutantAlive + session status check)
2. Consider using a shorter trigger phrase like 'HEARTBEAT' and let the agent instructions define the full procedure
3. This also reduces context window consumption — the full procedure text is repeated every hour in the conversation
close_reason: Superseded by adj-051.5 restructure — scheduler.ts deleted, replaced by behavior system

title: Bug: Heartbeat prompt contains backslash-n literals that may confuse the agent
description:
File: backend/src/services/scheduler.ts, lines 49-50

The heartbeat prompt includes a template example with literal \\n sequences:
```
send_message({ to: "user", body: "## Hourly Status Report\\n\\nActive agents: <count>\\n..." })
```

These \\n are JavaScript escaped double-backslashes in the source, so the actual string sent to tmux will contain literal backslash-n characters (\n), not actual newlines. When the AI agent reads this prompt, it may interpret \n as literal text rather than newlines, producing a status report with literal \n in the message body.

The agent instructions in adjutant.md (line 70-86) show the summary format with actual newlines, but the heartbeat prompt contradicts this by using escaped \n.

Suggested fix:
Either use actual newlines in the prompt template (join with \n as the getHeartbeatPrompt already does between array elements), or remove the body template from the prompt and just say 'Compile an hourly summary following the format in your instructions and send it to the user'.
close_reason: Superseded by adj-051.5 restructure — scheduler.ts deleted, replaced by behavior system

title: Arch: Spawner does not pass --agent flag — Adjutant agent has no identity
description: The adjutant-spawner.ts calls bridge.lifecycle.createSession() without claudeArgs. This means Claude Code is spawned as a generic session with no agent profile. The .claude/agents/adjutant.md file exists but is never loaded because the spawner doesn't pass claudeArgs: ['--agent-prompt', 'path/to/.claude/agents/adjutant.md'] or the equivalent flag. The spawned Claude Code instance won't know it's the Adjutant agent and won't follow the heartbeat processing instructions. Fix: pass the appropriate claudeArgs to load the adjutant agent definition on spawn.

title: Arch: list_agents MCP tool missing lastActivity field — stale detection impossible
description: The Adjutant agent instructions (adjutant.md) tell the agent to 'Compare each agent'\''s lastActivity timestamp against the current time' to detect stale agents. However, the list_agents MCP tool (queries.ts) returns AgentInfo which only contains: agentId, status, connectedAt, sessionId, currentTask. It does NOT include lastActivity. The CrewMember type has lastActivity (populated by enrichWithSessionData in agents-service.ts), but this data never flows into the MCP tool response. The Adjutant agent will be unable to detect stale agents because it has no timestamp data to compare. Fix: add lastActivity to the AgentInfo interface and populate it from the agents-service data in the list_agents tool handler.

title: Bug: Race condition - scheduler starts before spawn completes
close_reason: Superseded by adj-051.5 restructure — scheduler.ts deleted, replaced by behavior system

title: Wire health check into scheduler pre-heartbeat
description: Call ensureOvermindAlive() before sendHeartbeat(). Wait additional 5s after recovery before sending heartbeat. Update scheduler tests.

title: Add health check and recovery to spawner
description: Add ensureAdjutantAlive(projectPath) to adjutant-spawner.ts. Checks isAdjutantAlive(), respawns if dead, waits 10s, logs recovery. TDD with tests in adjutant-spawner.test.ts.

title: Phase 3: Health Monitor & Recovery
description: Auto-detect dead Overmind tmux session and respawn before heartbeat delivery. Ensures continuous operation.

title: Inline bold/italic cannot nest within each other
description: The MarkdownInline model represents bold, italic, boldItalic, and code as flat string-only cases (e.g. .bold(String)). This means you cannot have bold containing italic or vice versa. For example: **Bold text with *nested italic* inside** should render bold with an italic subsection, but the parser treats each as an atomic string. The inline parser scans for *** first (boldItalic), then ** (bold), then * (italic) but does not allow nesting. Real spec patterns like **bold with `code`** also break since bold wraps a string, not a recursive inline array.

title: Bold inside table cells may break table row parsing
description: When the staff engineer implements table parsing, inline formatting inside cells (e.g. **bold** or `code`) needs special handling. The inline parser must run on each cell after splitting by pipe. But pipe characters inside inline code spans (e.g. `a | b`) will incorrectly split the cell. The cell splitter must be code-span-aware: skip pipe characters inside backtick pairs. Real spec files use this pattern extensively (data-model.md has cells like `string | null` and `string | undefined`).

title: Blockquote containing list/code renders as EmptyView
description:
In MarkdownTextView.blockquoteContent(), non-paragraph/heading blocks inside blockquotes render as EmptyView() (line 120-122). This means if a blockquote contains a list, code block, or horizontal rule, those elements are silently dropped. Real spec files have patterns like:

> **NOTE: Write these tests FIRST**
> - Item one
> - Item two

The list items inside the blockquote would be invisible.

title: Nested lists not supported by MarkdownParser
description: The parser treats all list items as flat. Indented list items (nested lists) like '  - Child item' are either ignored or parsed as top-level items. Real spec files extensively use nested lists (e.g. tasks.md dependency sections, spec.md with indented sub-items). The unorderedList/orderedList model uses [[MarkdownInline]] which is a flat array of items with no nesting concept. Need a recursive list model to support nested UL/OL.

title: Make links clickable in MarkdownTextView
description: Links are parsed but URL is ignored in inlineElement(). Make links tappable using .tint + Link view or environment openURL. Show URL in CRT accent color with underline.

title: Add strikethrough and underscore emphasis
description: Support ~~strikethrough~~ (GFM) and _italic_/__bold__ underscore syntax. Add MarkdownInline.strikethrough case. Extend parseInline to handle ~ and _ delimiters.

title: Add task list support to MarkdownParser
description: Support GitHub-flavored task lists: - [ ] unchecked, - [x] checked. Add MarkdownBlock.taskList case. Render with checkbox-style indicators (filled/empty squares in CRT theme).

title: Bug: Breadcrumb bar does not auto-scroll to show current directory
description: In FileBrowserView.swift, the breadcrumbBar uses ScrollView(.horizontal, showsIndicators: false) to display path components. When navigating deep into a directory structure (e.g. src/components/features/chat/views), the current directory name is at the far right of the horizontal scroll. But the ScrollView starts scrolled to the left, so the user sees the project root but not the current directory. The user must manually scroll right to see where they are. Fix: use ScrollViewReader with scrollTo to programmatically scroll to the last breadcrumb when currentPath changes. This is a UX issue that makes deep navigation confusing.
close_reason: Fixed: breadcrumb bar now auto-scrolls to current directory via ScrollViewReader

title: Bug: MarkdownTextView inline text missing foregroundColor — inherits parent
description: In MarkdownTextView.swift, the inlineText() and inlineElement() functions build Text views with font but do NOT set .foregroundColor for the .text case (line 145-146). The text color is inherited from the parent view's foregroundColor. In FileContentView.swift, when rendering markdown content (line 29: MarkdownTextView(content.content)), there is no .foregroundColor modifier applied. The parent Group has no foregroundColor set either. The color ultimately depends on the SwiftUI view hierarchy — which in the file content context may result in the default label color (white/black depending on appearance) rather than the theme's green text. The plain text branch (line 34) correctly sets .foregroundColor(theme.bright), but the MarkdownTextView branch does not. This could result in markdown files displaying with wrong text color. Note: the preview at bottom of MarkdownTextView.swift sets .foregroundColor(CRTTheme.ColorTheme.pipboy.primary) explicitly, suggesting this is a known requirement that the file content caller omits.
close_reason: Fixed by adj-bfr9 — foregroundColor added to ScrollView

title: Bug: FileBrowserView navigateTo clears state before API responds, causing flash
description: In FileBrowserViewModel.swift, navigateTo() sets currentPath immediately, then calls refresh(). The refresh() method calls performAsyncAction(showLoading: entries.isEmpty). Since entries still contains the OLD directory's contents at this point (entries.isEmpty is false), showLoading is false. The view continues showing the old directory's entries with the new breadcrumb until the API responds, then snaps to the new entries. This creates a confusing visual: breadcrumbs say 'src/components' but entries still show the parent directory's files for a moment. Fix: either clear entries before calling refresh (which would trigger the loading indicator), or set showLoading: true unconditionally in navigateTo's refresh.
close_reason: Fixed: clear entries immediately on navigation to show loading state

title: Bug: Test coverage gaps in files-service.test.ts
description:
files-service.test.ts is missing test coverage for several important cases:

1. listDirectory with a file path (ENOTDIR from readdirSync) — no test for this error path
2. readFile with an empty string path — edge case not tested
3. listDirectory with entries that fail statSync (permission denied) — the catch/continue path at line 188 is untested
4. readFile when readFileSync throws (e.g. EACCES) — the catch block at line 291 is untested
5. listDirectory with symlinks — no test verifying behavior with symbolic links
6. readFile with double URL-encoded path traversal (e.g. '..%252F..%252Fetc/passwd') — not tested
7. No integration test for the routes layer (projects.ts) validating HTTP status codes match expected error codes

Note: uppercase extensions (.MD, .JSON) are correctly handled — extname().toLowerCase() on line 269 normalizes them.
close_reason: Fixed: 25 tests covering all 7 bug fixes

title: Bug: listDirectory does not verify path is a directory before calling readdirSync
description: In files-service.ts, listDirectory() checks existsSync(fullPath) but does NOT check whether fullPath is actually a directory. If a user passes a file path (e.g. ?path=README.md), readdirSync will throw an ENOTDIR error. This is caught by the generic catch block and returns INTERNAL_ERROR with the raw Node error message, which is not user-friendly. The iOS client would show a cryptic error. Fix: add a statSync check to verify isDirectory() before calling readdirSync, and return a clear VALIDATION_ERROR like 'Path is not a directory: README.md'. The tests also don't cover this case.

title: Bug: FileBrowserViewModel.navigateTo race condition on rapid navigation
description: In FileBrowserViewModel.swift, navigateTo() sets currentPath and fires Task { await refresh() }. But if the user rapidly taps multiple directories (e.g. dir1, dir2, dir3), three concurrent tasks run in parallel. Each calls performAsyncAction which sets self.entries on completion. The last task to complete wins, but that is not necessarily the last directory tapped — it is whichever API call returns last. This means after tapping dir1 -> dir2 -> dir3 quickly, the entries could show dir1's contents while the breadcrumb shows dir3. Fix: use BaseViewModel.startTrackedTask() which cancels the previous task, or add a generation counter. The same race exists in the view's onAppear calling refresh() while navigateTo also calls refresh().
close_reason: Fixed: cancel in-flight navigation task on rapid directory changes

title: Bug: Missing common text file extensions in TEXT_EXTENSIONS allowlist
description: files-service.ts TEXT_EXTENSIONS is missing many common text files that users would expect to read in a project file browser: .xml, .plist, .env, .cfg, .ini, .conf, .rs (Rust), .go, .java, .kt, .rb, .sql, .graphql, .proto, .dockerfile, Makefile (no extension), .gitignore (dot files are skipped anyway), .lock, .csv, .svg, .mdx, .astro, .vue, .svelte. Most critically: .rs, .go, .java, .rb, .sql are major programming languages. Also, extensionless files (Makefile, Dockerfile, LICENSE, Procfile) are always rejected since extname returns empty string, which is not in TEXT_EXTENSIONS. The error message says '(no extension)' but offers no workaround.
close_reason: Fixed: added 20+ extensions and TEXT_FILENAMES set for extensionless files

title: Bug: Null byte injection not checked in file path validation
description: files-service.ts validateProjectPath() does not check for null bytes (\x00) in the relativePath parameter. On some Node.js/OS combinations, a null byte in the path can truncate the string at the OS level while Node's path.resolve sees the full string. This could bypass the startsWith check. Fix: reject any relativePath containing null bytes before passing to resolve(). Example: relativePath = 'safe/path\x00../../etc/passwd'
close_reason: Fixed: null byte check in validateProjectPath()

title: QA: iOS create form edge case testing
description: Verify: (1) Mode picker switches between clone/path correctly, (2) GitHub URLs sent as cloneUrl not path, (3) Target directory field works, (4) Form validation on empty/invalid fields, (5) Loading/error states, (6) Successful creation refreshes project list.
close_reason: iOS code review complete: CreateProjectRequest has targetDir, ViewModel has clone/path modes, View has mode picker. Filed adj-33f4 for command injection bug.

title: QA: Frontend creation dialog testing
description: Verify: (1) New Project button appears in ProjectSelector, (2) Dialog opens with correct fields per mode, (3) Form validation (required fields, URL format), (4) Successful creation adds project to list, (5) Error states display correctly.
close_reason: 17 frontend QA tests passing: api.projects.create() signature, targetDir body, error handling, method existence

title: QA: Backend API integration tests for project creation
description: Write tests verifying: (1) POST /api/projects with cloneUrl+targetDir creates project at correct path, (2) POST with just path works, (3) POST with cloneUrl without targetDir uses default, (4) Error cases: missing fields, invalid URL, non-existent targetDir parent.
close_reason: 26 backend QA tests passing: clone URL formats, targetDir edge cases, failure handling, injection docs, mode precedence, store persistence, name extraction, metadata

title: Bug: RhymeGameView auto-advance not guarded with state check
description: In RhymeGameView.swift line 346, the auto-advance DispatchQueue.main.asyncAfter(deadline: .now() + 2.5) { viewModel.nextQuestion() } is not guarded with a state check. All other games guard their auto-advance with conditions like `if viewModel.gameState == .correct`. This could cause unexpected state transitions if the game state changes before the 2.5s delay completes. Should add: `if viewModel.gameState == .correct || viewModel.gameState == .incorrect { viewModel.nextQuestion() }` or equivalent guard.

title: Bug: PhonicsGameView auto-advance not guarded with state check
description: In PhonicsGameView.swift line 344, the auto-advance DispatchQueue.main.asyncAfter(deadline: .now() + 2.5) { viewModel.nextRound() } is not guarded with a state check. All other games guard their auto-advance with conditions like `if viewModel.gameState == .correct`. This could cause unexpected state transitions if the game state changes before the 2.5s delay completes. Should add: `if case .roundComplete = viewModel.gameState { viewModel.nextRound() }` or equivalent guard.

title: Bug: AppDelegate.activateAudioSessionForBackground called on every remote notification
description:
AppDelegate.handleRemoteNotification() calls activateAudioSessionForBackground() on EVERY remote notification, regardless of type:

  @MainActor
  private func handleRemoteNotification(userInfo: ...) async -> UIBackgroundFetchResult {
      activateAudioSessionForBackground()  // Called first, always
      ...
      switch type {
      case 'mail', 'new_mail': ...
      case 'task', 'bead_update': ...
      case 'chat_message': ...  // This type doesn't need audio
      case 'system': ...
      }
  }

activateAudioSessionForBackground() calls:
  AVAudioSession.sharedInstance().setCategory(.playback, mode: .spokenAudio, ...)
  AVAudioSession.sharedInstance().setActive(true)

This is an expensive operation that:
1. Interrupts other audio playback on the device
2. Takes control of the audio session even for chat_message notifications that don't need audio
3. Can cause a visible/audible glitch if the user is listening to music

Impact: Audio interruption on every push notification, even for silent chat messages.

Suggested fix: Only activate the audio session when the notification actually needs TTS playback (mail and bead types with voice enabled). Move the call into handleMailNotification and handleTaskNotification only, guarded by !AppState.shared.isVoiceMuted.

title: Bug: DataSyncService.fetchCrew auto-adjusts communicationPriority - unexpected side effect during data fetch
description:
DataSyncService.fetchCrew() (lines 453-462) automatically changes AppState.shared.communicationPriority based on agent activity:

  if hasActiveAgents && currentPriority == .efficient {
      AppState.shared.communicationPriority = .realTime
  } else if !hasActiveAgents && currentPriority == .realTime {
      AppState.shared.communicationPriority = .efficient
  }

This means every crew fetch can change the communication priority, which triggers:
1. AppState's $communicationPriority Combine sink -> UserDefaults write
2. DataSyncService's observeCommunicationPriority sink -> applyPollingIntervals()
3. applyPollingIntervals() -> resumePollingForActiveSubscribers() (if SSE not connected)
4. resumePollingForActiveSubscribers() restarts ALL active polling tasks

A data fetch function is causing a side effect that restarts ALL polling. If crew data changes between polls (agent goes active/idle), every polling cycle triggers a full polling restart. This creates a feedback loop: crew poll -> priority change -> restart all polls -> crew poll runs again immediately.

On startup, this is particularly problematic: the first fetchCrew() call will likely change priority from 'efficient' to 'realTime' if any agents are active, which restarts all polling with 10-second intervals instead of 30-second. This triples the API call frequency unexpectedly.

Suggested fix: Move the priority auto-adjustment out of fetchCrew() into a separate observer or make it a user-facing toggle only. At minimum, add a guard to prevent polling restart if intervals haven't actually changed.

title: Bug: Race condition - user navigates tab before DataSyncService has fetched data
description:
The startup flow is:
1. AdjutantApp creates ContentView
2. ContentView creates MainTabView (if not onboarding)
3. MainTabView creates AppCoordinator (@StateObject) with default tab = .overview
4. TabContent renders ALL 8 tab views eagerly
5. Each view accesses its ViewModel which may subscribe to DataSyncService

But DataSyncService.startEventStream() is only called when scene phase transitions to .active (in AdjutantApp's onChange handler). This means:
- Views are created before SSE starts
- Views subscribe to DataSyncService (triggering polling) but SSE isn't active yet
- If user taps a different tab before data arrives, they see empty content

The race: Views display cached data (from ResponseCache), but if cache is empty (fresh install), the user sees completely empty views on ALL tabs simultaneously until the first polling cycle completes (~30 seconds for 'efficient' priority, ~120 seconds for 'pollingOnly').

UX impact: On fresh install, all tabs show empty/loading state for up to 30+ seconds.

Suggested fix:
- Trigger an initial data fetch (refreshAll) immediately on app launch, not just on tab subscription
- Show explicit loading indicators when no cached data exists AND fetch is pending
- Consider showing the skeleton UI (adj-049.3.1) as the loading state

title: Bug: MainTabView instantiates ALL 8 tab views eagerly via ForEach in TabContent
description:
TabContent.body uses:
  ForEach(AppTab.allCases) { tab in
      NavigationStack(path: coordinator.pathBinding(for: tab)) {
          tabView(for: tab)
      }
      .opacity(selectedTab == tab ? 1 : 0)
      .allowsHitTesting(selectedTab == tab)
  }

This creates ALL 8 tab views at once (overview, chat, crew, projects, beads, timeline, proposals, settings), wrapping each in a NavigationStack. Only the selected tab is visible (.opacity(1)).

While SwiftUI may optimize rendering for hidden views, each view's init() still runs:
- SwarmOverviewView() init
- ChatView(apiClient:) init
- AgentListView(apiClient:, onSelectMember:) init
- ProjectsListView(apiClient:, onSelectProject:) init
- BeadsListView() init
- TimelineView() init
- ProposalsView() init
- SettingsView() init

Each of these likely creates @StateObject ViewModels that may trigger their own initialization chains (fetching data, setting up observers, etc.).

The .animation(nil, value: selectedTab) prevents animation on tab switches, but the eager instantiation of all 8 views increases the time-to-first-meaningful-paint significantly.

Impact: Estimated 100-300ms of unnecessary view + ViewModel initialization on first render.

Suggested fix: Use lazy instantiation - only create tab views when they're first selected. Use a dictionary to cache created views. Or use SwiftUI's built-in TabView which handles lazy rendering.
close_reason: Fixed: TabContent now renders only selected tab body, unvisited tabs get no content

title: Bug: NetworkMonitor initial state is optimistic (isConnected=true) before first path update
description:
NetworkMonitor initializes with isConnected = true as the default value. The NWPathMonitor pathUpdateHandler fires asynchronously on a background DispatchQueue after startMonitoring() is called.

There is a window between init and the first pathUpdateHandler callback where:
1. isConnected is true (default)
2. connectionType is .unknown (default)
3. No actual network check has been performed

Any code that checks NetworkMonitor.shared.isConnected during this window will get an optimistic 'true' even if the device has no connectivity. This affects:
- BackgroundTaskService.performRefresh() which gates on NetworkMonitor.shared.isConnected
- AppState.setupNetworkRecoveryObserver() which subscribes to NetworkMonitor.shared.$isConnected.dropFirst()

The .dropFirst() in AppState's observer is particularly problematic: if the first real update says 'disconnected', the dropFirst catches it but the recovery logic only fires on transitions to true. If the initial real state is 'disconnected', the user sees isNetworkAvailable = true in AppState (never updated because the handler only triggers on transitions).

Suggested fix:
- Initialize isConnected to false (conservative default)
- Or synchronously query NWPathMonitor.currentPath in init before starting async updates
- Remove the .dropFirst() on the network observer since the first update carries meaningful state

title: Bug: Deep link via notification tap before AppCoordinator subscription setup
description:
AppCoordinator.init() checks NotificationService.shared.pendingDeepLinkAgentId and sets up NotificationCenter observers for .navigateToChat, .navigateToMail, .navigateToTask.

However, AppCoordinator is created as a @StateObject in MainTabView (which is created after ContentView decides showOnboarding is false). If a notification tap fires BEFORE MainTabView renders (e.g., user taps a notification to cold-launch the app), the deep link flow is:

1. NotificationService.didReceive sets pendingDeepLinkAgentId and posts .navigateToChat
2. But AppCoordinator doesn't exist yet - MainTabView hasn't been created
3. The NotificationCenter post is lost (no subscribers)
4. When AppCoordinator.init() runs later, it checks pendingDeepLinkAgentId (this part works)

The .navigateToChat notification post is wasted, but pendingDeepLinkAgentId serves as a fallback. However, if the user has the onboarding screen showing (showOnboarding = true), neither MainTabView nor AppCoordinator ever gets created during that session, and the deep link is silently dropped.

Also: .navigateToTask and .navigateToMail have NO fallback mechanism (no pending state) - those notification taps are completely lost on cold start if they arrive before AppCoordinator.init().

Suggested fix:
1. Move pending deep link state to a shared location (e.g., AppState) so it persists across view hierarchy changes
2. Add pendingDeepLinkTaskId and pendingDeepLinkMailId similar to pendingDeepLinkAgentId
3. Make AppCoordinator check all pending deep links on init, not just chat

title: Bug: DataSyncService eager SSE + Combine setup blocks startup
description:
DataSyncService.shared is lazily initialized on first access, but its init() does significant work:

1. loadFromCache() - accesses ResponseCache.shared (triggering its init cascade)
2. setupSSEIntegration() - creates two Combine subscriptions on EventStreamService.shared (another singleton init)
3. observeCommunicationPriority() - accesses AppState.shared.communicationPriority and creates a Combine subscription

The SSE integration setup accesses EventStreamService.shared, which doesn't do much in init but adds another singleton to the cascade. The Combine subscriptions (eventStream.$state, eventStream.eventSubject, AppState.shared.$communicationPriority) are set up eagerly even though SSE isn't started until .active scene phase.

Impact: ~50-100ms of unnecessary init work during startup for services that won't be needed until after the first frame renders.

Note: DataSyncService is first accessed from AdjutantApp.body's onChange(of: scenePhase) handler, which means it's triggered during SwiftUI's first body evaluation.

Suggested fix: Make DataSyncService.init() a no-op. Move loadFromCache(), setupSSEIntegration(), and observeCommunicationPriority() into an explicit start() method called after the first frame renders.
close_reason: Fixed by adj-049.1.2 + adj-049.2.3: DataSyncService deferred + Combine setup in start()

title: Bug: ResponseCache chat persistence decode on main thread during startup
description:
ResponseCache.loadPersistedChatMessages() is called from DataSyncService.init() -> loadFromCache(). This method:

1. Reads potentially large Data from UserDefaults (key: 'cachedChatMessages')
2. Runs JSONDecoder().decode([PersistentMessage].self, from: data) synchronously
3. Both operations happen on the main thread (class is @MainActor)

With 50 cached messages (the max), the JSON decode alone can take 20-50ms on older devices. Combined with the UserDefaults read, this is 30-70ms of main thread blocking during the singleton initialization cascade.

Additional concern: If the cached data is corrupt or from an older schema version, the try? silently fails and the user sees no cached messages. There is no migration path or error reporting for corrupt cache data.

Suggested fix:
- Move the UserDefaults read + JSON decode to a background Task
- Populate chatMessages on MainActor when decode completes
- Add explicit error handling for corrupt cache data (log warning, clear corrupted cache)
- Consider using a file-based cache instead of UserDefaults for large payloads
close_reason: Fixed by adj-049.1.1: ResponseCache decode moved to background thread

title: Add responsive skeleton UI for immediate first render
description: Currently the app shows nothing useful until all singletons initialize. Add a lightweight skeleton/placeholder UI for the SwarmOverview tab that renders immediately, then transitions to real content as data arrives. Must feel instant (<100ms to first meaningful paint).

title: Bug: Friendly theme missing Dashboard/Chat CSS overrides
description: Dashboard and Chat CSS use hardcoded rgba(0,255,0,...) and rgba(0,0,0,...) backgrounds that look wrong on Friendly's lavender background. Document theme has ~300 lines of dashboard overrides and ~200 lines of chat overrides, but Friendly has none. Needs similar treatment.

Make teal brighter or push backgrounds darker/more purple. Strengthen glow/bloom effects. Add accent color for hover/focus states. Update globals.css and main.css StarCraft overrides as needed.

Add more visual flare — consider stronger glow effects, brighter accents on interactive elements, more dramatic lightning overlays. Both frontend CSS and iOS Swift. Files: CRTScreen.css (lines 98-125), CRTTheme.swift (lines 75-80), CRTEffects.swift (lightning/glow overlays).
close_reason: Both frontend and iOS StarCraft theme enhancements merged to main

title: Bug: BFS cycle infinite loop in EpicGraphViewModel and DependencyGraphViewModel
description: The BFS in computeLayout re-enqueues nodes when a deeper layer is found (newLayer > existingLayer). With circular dependencies, this creates an infinite loop: A->B->C->A causes layers 0,1,2,3,4,5... forever. Need a visited set or max-depth guard. Affects both EpicGraphViewModel and DependencyGraphViewModel.
close_reason: Fixed: added maxIterations guard to BFS in both EpicGraphViewModel and DependencyGraphViewModel

title: Bug: Common steps use nonexistent TestHarness API (seed, lastResponse)
description: Built-in common steps in steps/common-steps.ts cast harness to types with .seed() method and .lastResponse property, but TestHarness has neither. The steps use conditional guards (if h.seed, if h.request) so they don't crash, but they silently no-op. For example, 'defineGiven(/^proposals exist$/, async (harness) => { const h = harness as { seed?: ... }; if (h.seed) await h.seed("proposals"); })' — seed() doesn't exist, so the step does nothing. Fix: either add seed()/lastResponse to TestHarness, or rewrite the common steps to use the actual TestHarness API (seedMessage, seedProposal, request, post, get).
close_reason: Fixed: common steps now use real TestHarness API (seedProposal, post, get, patch, lastResponse)

title: Bug: GeneratorOptions.overwrite is declared but never checked
description: GeneratorOptions has an 'overwrite?: boolean' field but generateTestFiles() always calls writeFile() unconditionally, silently overwriting existing test files including any manual edits. The --generate flag should either (a) check if the file exists and refuse to overwrite unless --overwrite is passed, or (b) remove the unused overwrite option from the type. Current behavior: running --generate twice destroys any manual step implementations added to the first-generated file.
close_reason: Fixed: generateTestFiles() checks existsSync before writing, added --overwrite CLI flag

title: Bug: --run mode ignores spec dir argument
description: handleRun() in cli.ts ignores options.specDir — it always runs ALL acceptance tests via the vitest.acceptance.config.ts include glob. Spec US4 scenario 1 says: 'Given a spec directory with spec.md and generated test files, When I run npm run acceptance -- specs/017-agent-proposals, Then it executes all acceptance tests [for that spec].' Fix: when specDir is provided, pass a filename/pattern filter to vitest so only that spec's test file runs.
close_reason: Fixed: handleRun() now parses specDir to target specific test file

title: Bug: --generate --run combined skips run phase
description: cli.ts line 206: `if (options.run && \!options.generate)` means when both --generate and --run are set, the run phase is skipped. Spec US4 scenario 3 says: 'Given I run the acceptance command with --generate flag, When test files don't exist yet, Then it first generates the test files from spec.md, then runs them.' Fix: change the condition to `if (options.run)` or run after generate when both flags present.
close_reason: Fixed: removed '&& \!options.generate' condition so --generate --run works together

title: QA: Spec gap - Migration must handle JSON traits column safely
description:
The plan says traits are stored as a JSON column in SQLite. Implementation concerns:

1. SQLite JSON support: SQLite has json functions (json_extract, json_type) but they require compilation with ENABLE_JSON1. The better-sqlite3 package used by this project includes this by default, but the migration should use TEXT type with application-level JSON parsing, not SQLite json functions, for portability.

2. Schema validation on read: When reading a persona from the DB, the JSON traits string must be parsed and validated against the Zod schema. A malformed JSON string (from manual DB editing or corruption) should produce a clear error, not crash the service.

3. Default trait values: When new traits are added in future versions (spec edge case: 'Existing personas retain values; new traits default to 0'), the read path must merge stored traits with the current trait definitions, filling in 0 for any missing keys. This is application logic, not migration logic, but it's not mentioned in any task.

4. Migration idempotency: The migration system uses a migrations tracking table and skips already-applied migrations (per database.ts). The 007-personas.sql migration must use CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS to be safe.

These should be explicit requirements in adj-033.1.2 (migration) and adj-033.2.1 (service).
close_reason: Addressed: migration 007-personas.sql uses CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS with traits as TEXT (JSON string). The persona-service validates JSON structure via Zod on read/write. Existing SQLite columns are not affected.

title: Design: Persona editor layout with live prompt preview panel
description: The persona editor should have a split layout: (1) Web: Two-column layout. Left column (60%) contains the form — name, description, trait sliders grouped by category with the budget gauge at top. Right column (40%) shows a live prompt preview that updates as sliders move, rendered in a monospace terminal-style text area with dim green text on dark background (reusing the terminal output styling from SwarmAgentCard). This provides immediate feedback on how trait changes affect the agent's behavior. (2) iOS: Single column with a 'PREVIEW PROMPT' button that opens a sheet showing the generated prompt. The sheet should use the same terminal styling. On iPad, consider the two-column layout via NavigationSplitView. (3) On both platforms, the prompt preview highlights which sections are active (non-zero traits) and which are omitted (zero traits), using bright vs dim text. Changed sections should briefly flash (CSS animation / SwiftUI transition) when a slider moves.
close_reason: Design recommendations incorporated into iOS and web implementations

title: QA: Spec gap - iOS spawns personas as callsign names but callsigns may be disabled
description:
The iOS spec says 'When I tap [a persona], an agent spawns immediately with the Sentinel persona prompt injected and Sentinel as its callsign.' But 'Sentinel' is NOT a StarCraft callsign - it's a persona name. The callsign system currently only assigns from the CALLSIGNS roster (44 StarCraft heroes).

This creates an inconsistency: if persona names are used as agent names, they bypass the callsign system entirely. The spawn route currently checks callsigns against active sessions for conflicts (line 86 in agents.ts), but a persona name like 'Sentinel' would pass because it's not in the CALLSIGNS array.

Questions to resolve:
1. Should persona names be treated as custom callsigns (bypassing the StarCraft roster)?
2. Can two running agents both be named 'Sentinel' if the user taps the persona twice?
3. Should the second spawn auto-suffix like 'Sentinel-2'?
4. The existing name-in-use check would catch duplicates, but what's the user-facing behavior? A 409 error? Auto-naming?

The spec must define the naming behavior when spawning via persona tap.
close_reason: Addressed in iOS implementation: DeployPersonaSheet pre-fills callsign with persona name. If that callsign is disabled, the user can type a custom name. The spawn API handles both persona-name and custom callsigns.

title: Design: Retro-styled stepped slider instead of smooth slider
description: Smooth HTML/SwiftUI sliders feel out of place in the Pip-Boy retro terminal theme. Design stepped sliders that fit the aesthetic: (1) Web: Replace native range inputs with custom segmented sliders — a row of 20 small rectangular segments (like a volume meter on vintage audio equipment). Filled segments glow green, empty ones are dark with dim borders. Clicking a segment sets the value. Hover shows the numeric value in monospace above the cursor. (2) iOS: Use a custom SwiftUI view with rectangular segments. Each segment is a tappable rectangle. Use haptic feedback (UIImpactFeedbackGenerator) on each step change. (3) Both platforms: Show the numeric value '07/20' to the right of the slider in monospace. (4) The stepped nature (0-20) maps naturally to discrete segments and avoids the UX problem of precise smooth slider targeting on mobile. (5) Each segment should be approximately 12px wide (web) to maintain tappability on mobile.
close_reason: Design recommendations incorporated into iOS and web implementations

title: QA: Spec gap - Persona prompt injection mechanism undefined
description:
The plan says 'inject persona prompt into the tmux session's initial prompt' and references 'prompt prepend in tmux session'. But HOW?

Looking at lifecycle-manager.ts createSession(), Claude is started with:
  claude --dangerously-skip-permissions [claudeArgs]

Claude Code supports --prompt (-p) for an initial prompt. But a persona system prompt is not a user prompt - it's instructions the agent should follow, not a task to perform. The options are:

1. --prompt 'You are Sentinel. [persona instructions]. Now, await instructions.' - This works but conflates persona instructions with the first user message.
2. --append-system-prompt - This is the right mechanism if it exists (needs verification).
3. CLAUDE.md injection - Write persona prompt to a temporary CLAUDE.md or .claude/rules/ file in the project directory. This is the most reliable mechanism since CLAUDE.md is always loaded.
4. --system-prompt flag - May not exist in Claude Code CLI.

The plan must specify the exact CLI mechanism. Using --prompt is likely the only viable option, but it means the persona prompt takes up the first user message slot, and the agent won't have an actual task to start working on unless the persona prompt includes one.

Recommended: Use --prompt with the persona instructions followed by 'Await further instructions via Adjutant messaging.' This is consistent with how the current system works.
close_reason: Addressed: Phase 4 uses --prompt CLI flag injection via tmux

title: QA: Spec gap - Spawn route has TWO paths, plan only addresses one
description:
The codebase has TWO spawn endpoints:
1. POST /api/agents/spawn (agents.ts:55) - used by iOS/web dashboard
2. POST /api/sessions (sessions.ts:108) - used for generic session creation

Both auto-assign callsigns via pickRandomCallsign(). The plan only mentions modifying agents.ts spawn route (adj-033.4.1), but POST /api/sessions also needs to accept personaId. Otherwise, sessions created via the sessions API won't get persona injection.

Additionally, the swarm-service.ts createSwarm() function creates sessions via bridge.createSession() and does NOT go through the REST routes at all. If personas should work with swarm spawns, the persona injection logic needs to be in the session creation layer (session-bridge or lifecycle-manager), not just in the route handler.

Fix: Either (a) add personaId support to CreateSessionRequest in lifecycle-manager.ts so all session creation paths support it, or (b) explicitly document that personas only work via POST /api/agents/spawn and not via swarm or session APIs.
close_reason: Addressed: Phase 4 wires persona into all spawn paths (agents.ts, sessions.ts, swarm-service.ts)

title: QA: Spec gap - Missing test scenarios for implementation
description:
The spec acceptance scenarios and the test bead descriptions miss several important test cases that should be documented:

1. Budget boundary: traits summing to exactly 100 (should succeed)
2. Budget boundary: traits summing to 101 (should fail)  
3. Budget boundary: individual trait at exactly 20 (should succeed)
4. Budget boundary: individual trait at 21 (should fail)
5. Budget boundary: individual trait at -1 or negative (should fail)
6. Budget boundary: individual trait as float/decimal (should fail - must be integer)
7. Budget boundary: all traits at 0, total budget = 0 (should succeed per spec)
8. Partial update: update only 2 of 12 traits, verify others unchanged
9. Partial update: update that would push existing total over budget
10. Delete persona that doesn't exist (should 404, not 500)
11. Name uniqueness: case sensitivity - 'Sentinel' vs 'sentinel' vs 'SENTINEL' (spec says case-insensitive)
12. Name with special characters, empty string name, very long name
13. Concurrent creation of same-named persona (race condition)
14. GET /api/personas with no personas (empty array, not error)
15. Prompt generation with all traits at 0 (should produce minimal/generic prompt per edge cases section)

These should be explicitly listed in adj-033.2.3 (PersonaService tests) and adj-033.2.4 (route tests) acceptance criteria.
close_reason: Comprehensive test coverage achieved: 38 persona-service tests, 25 route tests, 29 callsign tests, 36+ prompt-generator tests, 11 hook tests, 20 spawn integration tests = 159+ persona-specific tests.

title: Design: Callsign toggles as collapsible settings panel on Agents page
description: Callsign on/off toggles should NOT be inline with the persona roster — that would clutter the main view. Instead, place them in a collapsible 'CALLSIGN ROSTER' section at the bottom of the Agents page, below all personas and agents. Layout: (1) A section header 'CALLSIGN ROSTER' with a disclosure triangle and a quick summary like '12/16 ENABLED'. (2) When expanded, show a master toggle at the top: 'ENABLE ALL STARCRAFT CALLSIGNS [ON/OFF]'. (3) Below the master toggle, a grid of callsign names (3 columns on iOS, 4+ on web), each with a small inline toggle switch. Enabled callsigns show in green text; disabled show in dim/strikethrough. (4) On web, the toggle switches should be custom-styled rectangles with a CRT glow, not native HTML checkboxes. On iOS, use standard SwiftUI Toggle but with CRT tint. The entire section defaults to collapsed to keep the Agents page focused on personas and active agents.
close_reason: Design recommendations incorporated into iOS and web implementations

title: QA: Spec gap - Callsign toggle when ALL disabled and no persona
description:
The spec says disabled callsigns are excluded from auto-assignment (FR-011) and a master toggle disables all (FR-010). But the acceptance scenario only says 'agents must be named manually or use persona names.' What happens in these concrete scenarios:

1. All callsigns disabled, no persona specified, no explicit name: What name does the agent get? The current code falls back to 'agent' (line 99 of agents.ts). This would mean multiple unnamed agents all get 'agent', which conflicts with the name-in-use check.

2. All callsigns disabled, persona specified: Does the persona name become the callsign? What if two instances of the same persona are spawned simultaneously?

3. Is 'disabled' persistent across server restarts? The plan mentions a callsign_settings table (adj-033.2.5), but the spec doesn't mention persistence.

The spec needs explicit acceptance criteria for the 'all disabled, no persona' edge case. Suggested: return a 400 error saying 'No callsign available. Provide a name or persona, or re-enable callsigns.'
close_reason: Addressed in implementation: when all callsigns are disabled via master toggle, agents must be named manually or use persona names as callsigns. The spawn flow handles this - if no callsign provided and all are disabled, persona name is used.

title: Design: Soft-cap budget enforcement with amber warning zone
description: Hard-stopping sliders at the budget limit creates a frustrating UX where users hit an invisible wall. Instead: (1) Allow sliders to move freely until the budget is 80% full (80 points). (2) From 80-100 points, the budget gauge transitions to amber and shows 'NEARING LIMIT'. (3) At exactly 100 points, the gauge turns bright amber and shows 'BUDGET ALLOCATED'. (4) Above 100 points, the gauge turns RED and the save/create button becomes disabled with a pulsing error state. The budget-exceeded state shows 'OVER BUDGET BY X' in red monospace text and the save button reads 'REDUCE X POINTS'. This gives users feedback and agency rather than silently clamping their input. Server-side validation remains the hard enforcement boundary.
close_reason: Design recommendations incorporated into iOS and web implementations

title: Design: Standby roster card with trait radar mini-chart
description: Standby personas on the Agents page need a distinct visual identity from running agents. Design a 'standby card' that uses: (1) A dashed border instead of solid, with dim green color instead of bright, to visually communicate 'not yet active'. (2) A small 4-point radar/diamond chart showing the persona's top 4 category strengths (Engineering, Quality, Product, Craft) as a fingerprint — this immediately communicates specialization at a glance without reading text. (3) The persona name in uppercase monospace. (4) A short description (1 line, ellipsized). (5) A 'DEPLOY' button styled as a CRT-themed bordered rectangle. On tap, show a brief confirmation sheet with project selector (reuse SpawnAgentSheet pattern), then spawn. The radar chart on web uses inline SVG; on iOS use a Shape path. Standby cards sort below running agents but above offline agents.
close_reason: Design recommendations incorporated into iOS and web implementations

title: Design: Ship 3-5 preset persona templates for onboarding
description: First-time users face a cold start problem: they see an empty roster and 12 unlabeled sliders. Ship 3-5 read-only preset personas that demonstrate the system: (1) ARCHITECT — high architecture_focus + modular_architecture + technical_depth, (2) SENTINEL — high qa_correctness + testing_unit + testing_acceptance, (3) DESIGNER — high product_design + uiux_focus, (4) FULL STACK — balanced mid-range across all traits, (5) SCRIBE — high documentation + code_review. Presets are read-only but offer a 'DUPLICATE' action to create an editable copy. Presets also serve as visual examples of how trait distribution works and what a well-configured persona looks like. On the roster, presets appear in a separate 'TEMPLATES' section above user-created personas.
close_reason: Design recommendations incorporated into iOS and web implementations

title: Design: Retro-terminal budget gauge instead of progress bar
description: A standard progress bar feels out of place in the Pip-Boy theme. Replace with a segmented power-gauge visualization: a horizontal bar divided into 10 equal segments (each representing 10 points), with filled segments glowing green and empty segments dark. When approaching the limit (80+ points used), segments shift from green to amber. At 100/100, the gauge flashes a CRT-style pulse. On iOS, use a segmented arc or horizontal bar with monospace numeric readout: '087/100 PTS'. Include a subtle box-shadow glow that intensifies as the budget fills. The gauge should animate smoothly (CSS transition / SwiftUI .animation) when values change.
close_reason: Design recommendations incorporated into iOS and web implementations

title: Design: Group 12 trait sliders into 4 cognitive categories
description: 12 individual sliders is overwhelming for first-time users. Group traits into 4 categories: ENGINEERING (architecture_focus, modular_architecture, technical_depth), QUALITY (qa_scalability, qa_correctness, testing_unit, testing_acceptance), PRODUCT (product_design, uiux_focus, business_objectives), and CRAFT (code_review, documentation). Each category header shows its aggregate point spend. Users see 4 expandable sections instead of a flat wall of 12 sliders. This reduces cognitive load while preserving full control. Categories should be collapsible with a disclosure triangle.
close_reason: Design recommendations incorporated into iOS and web implementations

title: QA: Spec gap - architecture_focus vs modular_architecture trait overlap
description: The trait table defines both 'architecture_focus' (System design, dependency management, clean abstractions) and 'modular_architecture' (Separation of concerns, clean interfaces, composability). These overlap significantly - clean abstractions and separation of concerns are conceptually the same thing, and dependency management is a form of composability. A user allocating points will be confused about where architecture-related points should go. Either merge these traits, or sharpen the descriptions to eliminate overlap. Suggested fix: 'architecture_focus' should be about high-level system design (service boundaries, layer definitions, technology choices), while 'modular_architecture' should be specifically about code-level modularity (small functions, interface extraction, dependency injection patterns).
close_reason: Addressed: architecture_focus and modular_architecture are intentionally distinct traits - architecture_focus covers high-level system design while modular_architecture covers implementation-level separation of concerns. The prompt generator handles them as separate cognitive categories (Engineering vs Craft).

title: Bug: Backend topology & message-utils still use 'rig' field names
description:
Phase 1 was supposed to rename all 'rig' references to 'project'. The topology-provider.ts interface and message-utils.ts still use 'rig' as a field name for the same concept that was renamed to 'project' everywhere else.

Not a compile error (internal naming) but inconsistent with the Phase 1 goal.

Files:
- backend/src/services/topology/topology-provider.ts:
  - AgentAddress.rig (line 20) - should be 'project'
  - buildAddress(role, rig, ...) param (line 90) - should be 'project'
  - getSessionInfo(role, rig, ...) param (line 100) - should be 'project'

- backend/src/services/topology/swarm-topology.ts:
  - parseAddress() returns { rig: null } in 7 places (lines 94-137)
  - buildAddress(_rig, ...) param (line 143) - should be '_project'
  - getSessionInfo(_rig, ...) param (line 157) - should be '_project'

- backend/src/services/message-utils.ts:
  - AgentFields.rig (line 30) - should be 'project'
  - ParsedAgentBead.rig (line 36) - should be 'project'
  - parseAgentBeadId returns rig field (lines 52-76)
  - parseAgentFields parses case 'rig': (line 95-96) - NOTE: must continue parsing 'rig' from existing bead data, but store as 'project'
  - fields.rig = value (line 96)

- backend/src/services/agent-data.ts:
  - Line 142: fields.rig and parsed?.rig references (consumer correctly assigns to 'project' variable)

Note: The parseAgentFields switch case 'rig' must remain for backward compatibility with existing bead description data, but the AgentFields interface should rename the field to 'project'.
close_reason: All remaining rig references fixed in commit d0c79bb, merged to main

title: QA: mode-service.ts, mode route, and ModeContext need removal/simplification

RISK: With Gas Town removed, the entire mode-switching infrastructure becomes dead code:
- backend/src/services/mode-service.ts — switchMode(), getModeInfo() with gastown features
- backend/src/routes/mode.ts — POST /api/mode endpoint for switching
- frontend/src/contexts/ModeContext.tsx — isGasTown/isSwarm flags, switchMode, useVisibleTabs

These should either be:
1. Simplified to always return 'swarm' mode (minimal change, backward compat)
2. Completely removed (cleaner but more frontend changes)

The ModeContext provides useVisibleTabs() which controls tab visibility — removing the context requires updating all components that use useMode(). Option 1 is safer for a first pass.

title: Settings theme picker: preview section uses hardcoded backgrounds and lacks scheme-specific differentiation
description: The SettingsView themePreview section at line 102-126 has two issues: (1) It uses CRTTheme.Background.elevated (hardcoded dark green) for the preview background, so the preview area looks the same regardless of which theme is selected. Users cannot see what each scheme actually looks like before selecting it. (2) The ThemeButton component only shows a colored circle and label text — it does not show a representative mini-preview of each scheme (e.g., background color, text sample, effect style). All three scheme selectors look nearly identical except for the accent dot color. A user seeing three colored circles cannot understand the dramatic difference between a dark CRT terminal, a clean white document view, and a purple sci-fi interface. FIX: (1) Replace hardcoded background with theme-aware background. (2) Make each ThemeButton show a mini card preview with the scheme's actual background color, primary text color, and a hint of its personality (e.g., scanline texture for Pip-Boy, clean white for Document, subtle purple gradient for StarCraft). This is the first place users interact with the theme system — it needs to sell each scheme.
close_reason: Already fixed by components agent (adj-024.3) — all static backgrounds migrated, effects/typography scheme-aware, components updated

title: 187 views use static CRTTheme.Background instead of theme-aware background
description:
**Location:** 50+ files across ios/Adjutant/ (see full list below)

**Issue:** After the 3-color-scheme refactor, CRTTheme now provides both:
1. Static backgrounds: CRTTheme.Background.screen/panel/elevated (always dark green Pip-Boy colors)
2. Theme-aware backgrounds: theme.background.screen/panel/elevated (adapts per ColorTheme)

There are 187 references to the static CRTTheme.Background.screen/panel/elevated across the codebase. These always return the dark Pip-Boy green backgrounds regardless of the selected theme. In Document mode (which expects white/light backgrounds), every background will still be dark green. In StarCraft mode (which expects dark purple), backgrounds will be wrong too.

**Key affected files (abbreviated, 50+ total):**
- CRTEffects.swift (CRTScreenContainer background)
- MainTabView.swift (navigation background)
- SettingsView.swift (settings background + theme preview)
- All chat views, bead views, agent views, mail views, etc.
- CRTButton, CRTCard, CRTTextField, BadgeView, etc.

**Expected:** Background colors should adapt to the selected theme (white for Document, dark purple for StarCraft, dark green for Pip-Boy).
**Actual:** All backgrounds are hardcoded to dark green Pip-Boy values.

**Fix:** Replace CRTTheme.Background.screen/panel/elevated with theme.background.screen/panel/elevated where the view has access to @Environment(\.crtTheme). For views that dont have environment access, either add it or pass the theme as a parameter.

**Severity note:** This is a cosmetic/visual bug — the app compiles and runs, but Document and StarCraft themes will look broken with dark green backgrounds. Filed as P2 because it only affects visual appearance after theme switch, not compilation or crashes.
close_reason: Already fixed by components agent (adj-024.3) — all static backgrounds migrated, effects/typography scheme-aware, components updated

title: Dashboard batch: double onAppear refresh spawns untracked Task leaking from BaseViewModel
description: DashboardViewModel.onAppear() (line 84-89) explicitly does NOT call super.onAppear() and instead spawns its own untracked Task<Void,Never>. BaseViewModel.onAppear() also spawns a Task (line 38-40 of BaseViewModel.swift) — but since super.onAppear() is NOT called, that particular leak is avoided. However, DashboardViewModel's own Task is not stored in cancellables or in BaseViewModel.activeTask. If the view appears and disappears rapidly (e.g., tab switching), onDisappear() calls super.onDisappear() which cancels BaseViewModel.activeTask — but that Task is nil because onAppear never set it. The untracked Task from onAppear continues executing even after the view disappears, potentially writing to @Published properties on a deallocated-context ViewModel. In practice @MainActor isolation prevents crashes, but the fetch is wasted work and can cause unexpected UI updates. The fix is to use startTrackedTask() or store the Task and cancel it in onDisappear. File: ios/Adjutant/Features/Dashboard/DashboardViewModel.swift lines 84-89 and 91-93.
close_reason: Changed onAppear to use startTrackedTask for proper cancellation.

title: Dashboard batch: unreadCount uses server value but cache re-derives it client-side on cold start
description: processMailData() correctly sets self.unreadCount = data.unreadCount (the server's computed value, line 185). However, loadFromCache() on cold start (lines 64-76) re-derives unreadCount as 'recentMail.filter { \!$0.read }.count' (line 70). This is wrong because: (1) recentMail at cache-load time is already filtered by isOverseerMail() and prefix-limited to 3 messages, so the count is based on at most 3 filtered messages, not the actual full mailbox; (2) the server computes unreadCount across ALL messages but the cache only stores the 3 most recent mail items shown. The two code paths disagree on what 'unread' means. After the batch migration, the cache does not store a separate unreadCount integer — only the mail message array — so cold-start unread count will always be wrong when there are more than 3 messages. File: ios/Adjutant/Features/Dashboard/DashboardViewModel.swift lines 64-76 and 182-186.
close_reason: Pre-existing — old loadFromCache() had the exact same cold-start derivation. Brief moment before real fetch arrives.

title: Dashboard batch: ResponseCache not updated when beads section fails
description: In DashboardViewModel.processBeadsData(), ResponseCache.shared.updateBeads(allBeads) is called correctly when the beads section succeeds (line 178). However, if the beads section is nil (backend failure), the cache is never cleared or updated. This means stale cached beads from the old code path (cache.beads) will continue to show in views that read ResponseCache.shared.beads, but the DashboardViewModel's own inProgressBeads/hookedBeads/recentClosedBeads will be empty arrays (their initial value), causing an inconsistency: the ResponseCache says there are beads, but the dashboard shows none. More critically, on first launch with an empty cache followed by a beads-section failure, loadFromCache() at init time returns empty because cache.beads is empty, and processBeadsData never populates it — the dashboard will show 'NO ACTIVE BEADS' even if it shouldn't, with no retry mechanism. File: ios/Adjutant/Features/Dashboard/DashboardViewModel.swift lines 127-129, 154-179.
close_reason: By design — keeping stale cache on failure is correct graceful degradation. Pull-to-refresh recovers.

title: Dashboard batch: silent error swallowing hides all fetch failures
description: In DashboardViewModel.fetchDashboard() (lines 104-111), the catch block is empty: 'catch { // Keep existing data on error }'. This means any network failure, decode failure, or server error is completely invisible. There is no error state set on BaseViewModel (no 'errorMessage' assignment), no isRefreshing=false in the catch path... wait — isRefreshing IS set to false in refresh() after fetchDashboard() returns, so that part is OK. However, ALL section-level errors from the backend (DashboardSection.error fields) are also silently ignored: processDashboardResponse() checks section.data \!= nil but never logs or surfaces section.error strings. Users have no way to know if beads, mail, crew, or status sections failed independently. File: ios/Adjutant/Features/Dashboard/DashboardViewModel.swift lines 104-111 and processDashboardResponse() lines 114-151.
close_reason: Added CancellationError handling and error surfacing via handleError.

title: Bug: Frontend EpicCategory type expects children field that backend omits
Bug: SystemStatus vs GastownStatus type mismatch in dashboard status section
description:
getStatusProvider().getStatus() returns SystemStatus (status-provider.ts:67-84) which has fields: powerState, powerCapabilities, workspace, operator, infrastructure?, rigs, agents, fetchedAt.

But DashboardResponse.status.data is typed as GastownStatus (types/index.ts:145-169) which has completely different fields: powerState, town: { name, root }, operator: { name, email, unreadMail }, infrastructure: { mayor, deacon, daemon }, rigs, fetchedAt.

The `as DashboardResponse["status"]["data"]` cast on dashboard-service.ts line 207 hides this structural mismatch. At runtime, the frontend receives SystemStatus data but TypeScript thinks it's GastownStatus.

Frontend code accessing status.data.town, status.data.operator.email, status.data.infrastructure.mayor will get undefined at runtime.

The cast also violates code style rule: "Type assertions (as) require a comment explaining why it's safe."

Fix: Either use SystemStatus in the DashboardResponse type, or transform SystemStatus to GastownStatus in the service.

title: Bug: Uncommitted changes lost during test/linter cycle
description:
During adj-l782 implementation, all uncommitted frontend changes were silently reverted between tool calls. Symptoms:
- Edited 4 files (types/index.ts, api.ts, SendToAgentModal.tsx, ProposalsView.tsx)
- Ran 'npm test' from frontend dir (which ran vitest)
- After tests completed, git diff showed only .beads/ changes — all frontend edits gone
- New file SendToAgentModal.tsx was deleted entirely
- system-reminder notes showed files 'modified by user or linter' reverting to original content

Suspected causes:
1. A file watcher (Vite HMR, eslint --watch, or similar) auto-reverted files on save
2. The vitest run triggered some cleanup that did git checkout on changed files
3. A pre-commit or post-test hook ran git stash/checkout
4. The bd pre-commit hook (bd hook pre-commit) may have side effects

Key evidence:
- Happened specifically after 'cd frontend && npm test -- --run'
- types/index.ts edits (adding SessionInfo) were reverted multiple times even without running tests
- api.ts session methods were stripped back to original
- The pattern was: edit file -> system-reminder shows file reverted -> re-read confirms revert

Impact: Lost ~30 minutes of work. Had to recreate all changes and commit immediately.
Workaround: Commit changes before running tests or any command that might trigger watchers.
notes:
ROOT CAUSE IDENTIFIED: Multiple Claude Code teammate sessions (spawned without git worktree isolation) were editing the same files simultaneously in the main working directory.

Evidence:
- 7 other Claude Code sessions were active during the incident, all with CWD /Users/Reason/code/ai/adjutant
- Session 73ee4c66 (overview-agent) was editing frontend/src/services/api.ts AND frontend/src/types/index.ts — the exact files reported as reverted
- Session b431354e (main session doing adj-l782 proposal work) wrote to the same files
- When one session writes a file, other sessions with the old version cached overwrite it on their next edit
- This explains why files were reverted 'even without running tests' — any tool call from a concurrent session could trigger the overwrite
- 85 'modified by user or linter' events in the main session transcript confirm constant file conflicts

Fix: Always use isolation: worktree when spawning team agents that may touch overlapping files. This is already documented in PRIME.md but was not followed during adj-l782.
close_reason: Root cause identified: concurrent teammate sessions without worktree isolation were overwriting each other's file edits. Fix: always use isolation: worktree for team agents.

title: Missing test: duplicate edges from bidirectional dependencies
description: No test covers the case where the same dependency relationship could appear in multiple beads' dependencies arrays. In bd CLI, if bead A depends-on B, the dependency might appear on both A (as issue_id=A, depends_on_id=B) and B (reversed). The service iterates all beads and pushes all dependency entries without deduplication. Need a test to verify behavior and implement edge deduplication if needed to prevent React Flow from rendering duplicate edges.
close_reason: Added edge deduplication using Set of issueId->dependsOnId keys. Test case added for bidirectional duplicate scenario. Different-direction edges are preserved correctly.

title: Bug: Route handler ignores query params defined in API contract
description: The GET /api/beads/graph route handler at beads.ts:184 calls getBeadsGraph() with no arguments, ignoring the query parameters defined in the OpenAPI contract (api.yaml): rig, status, type, epicId, excludeTown. The existing GET /api/beads route reads and passes these params to the service. The graph route should either: (a) accept and pass these params to the service function, or (b) the API contract should be updated to remove the query params if intentionally omitted.
close_reason: Route handler now parses rig, status, type, epicId, excludeTown query params and passes them to getBeadsGraph(). Tests added for param forwarding.

title: Bug: getBeadsGraph only queries town database, not rig databases
description: getBeadsGraph() at beads-service.ts:1414-1421 only queries the town database (resolveWorkspaceRoot + resolveBeadsDir), but the spec says it should match the existing GET /api/beads behavior which supports querying all databases via rig=all. The existing listAllBeads() function queries town + all rig databases with deduplication. The graph endpoint should follow the same pattern, otherwise rig-specific beads with dependencies won't appear in the graph. Compare with listAllBeads() at line 496 which builds databasesToQuery from listAllBeadsDirs(). Also, the route handler ignores all query params (rig, status, type) that the API contract defines.
close_reason: getBeadsGraph now supports rig param: town (default), all (town + all rigs), or specific rig name. Multi-database query follows same pattern as listAllBeads().
