Implement a JSONL reader that can: (1) parse session logs for historical cost calculation using Anthropic's published pricing, (2) reconcile against statusline-reported totals, (3) provide token-level attribution per API call. Use statusline for real-time display, JSONL for authoritative records.
close_reason: All sub-tasks completed and merged to main

title: Bug: extractStatusBarCost only matches first status bar line — misses multi-session tmux
description: extractStatusBarCost() in session-connector.ts iterates lines and returns on the FIRST match of the STATUS_BAR pattern (NN% followed by the prompt chevrons). In a tmux pane with split views or if the capture includes multiple status bar renderings (e.g., pane resized), only the first match is returned. If the first match is stale (from a previous render), the cost data could be outdated. Should match the LAST occurrence instead of the first. Change: iterate lines in reverse, or collect all matches and take the last one.
close_reason: Low priority P3 — extractStatusBarCost first-match is correct for single-session panes

title: iOS/frontend context color thresholds are off-by-one at boundaries
description:
The frontend and iOS use different boundary logic for context usage color thresholds, causing inconsistent colors at exact boundary values (50%, 75%, 90%).

**Frontend (SwarmAgentCard.tsx getContextColor):**
- `pct > 90` -> red, `pct > 75` -> amber, `pct > 50` -> yellow-green, else -> green
- So: 50=green, 75=yellow-green, 90=amber

**iOS (AgentRowView.swift contextColor):**
- `<50` -> green, `50..<75` -> warning, `75..<90` -> orange, `>=90` -> red
- So: 50=warning, 75=orange, 90=red

At pct=50: frontend=green, iOS=yellow. At pct=75: frontend=yellow-green, iOS=orange. At pct=90: frontend=amber, iOS=red.

**Fix:** Align one to match the other. The iOS ranges are arguably more intuitive (50 means "half full" so should be warning). Recommend changing frontend to use `>=` thresholds: `pct >= 90` red, `pct >= 75` amber, `pct >= 50` yellow-green, else green.

title: Bug: UI_KEYWORDS regex false-positive on 'views' and 'filters' in non-UI contexts
description: pattern-detector.ts line 247: UI_KEYWORDS includes 'views?' and 'filters?' which match common API/data terms. Example: 'the user views proposals' -> UI. But 'GET /api/proposals is called and views are returned' would also match 'views', incorrectly classifying an API scenario as ui-only. Similarly, 'filters' in 'GET /api/items?filters=active' would match. Since api-testable has higher priority, this mostly manifests when there's no API pattern in When but Then/Given mentions 'views' or 'filters'.
close_reason: Low risk: api-testable classification has higher priority than ui-only, so false positives on 'views'/'filters' only matter when there's no API pattern — rare and non-blocking.

title: Bug: generated test descriptions are ungrammatical (should + conjugated verb)
description: test-generator.ts generateItDescription(): strips leading pronouns then prepends 'should'. Result: 'should only pending proposals are returned' instead of 'should return only pending proposals'. The grammar fix only handles 'is/are' at start but not deeper in the sentence. See generated file line 42: it('should only pending proposals are returned sorted by newest first'). adj-058.4 may already cover this.
close_reason: Duplicate of adj-058.4 (already fixed by engineer-2 with descriptionFromThen)

title: Bug: step registry global state leaks between test suites
description: step-registry.ts uses module-level mutable array. common-steps.ts registers at import time. clearSteps() resets but cached module won't re-register. Test suites that clear and re-import lose common steps permanently.
close_reason: By design: clearSteps() is for test isolation. In production, steps stay registered. Module cache behavior is expected.

title: Bug: query param parsing truncates values containing = sign
description: File: backend/src/acceptance/pattern-detector.ts, line 79. Query parameter parsing uses pair.split('=') which destructures as [key, value]. Since split only returns the first two segments, a query like ?filter=a=b produces {filter: 'a'} instead of {filter: 'a=b'}. Fix: use pair.split('=', 2) or pair.indexOf('=') + substring to preserve the full value.

title: DESIGN: Dual storage of coordinator decisions in adjutant_decisions AND events tables
description: After the bridge is connected, every logDecision call will write to BOTH adjutant_decisions table (via state-store.ts line 174-177) AND the events table (via eventStore.insertEvent). This creates dual storage with no referential link between them. The adjutant_decisions table has configurable pruning (pruneOldDecisions), while the events table prunes at 7 days (pruneOldEvents). The tables have different schemas (decisions has outcome/outcome_at fields, events has messageId/beadId). Questions to resolve: (1) Should adjutant_decisions be deprecated in favor of events table with a coordinator_action eventType? (2) Should the events row reference the decision ID? (3) Are the different retention policies intentional or a bug?
close_reason: Acceptable by design — decisions table is the source of truth with 30d retention, events table is the display layer with 7d retention. Different purposes.

title: EDGE CASE: rebalance_work emits N events per bead — timeline noise for bulk operations
description: In coordination.ts line 414, logDecision is called inside a for-loop for each bead being rebalanced. When the bridge connects logDecision to eventStore.insertEvent, a rebalance of 10 beads will emit 10 separate coordinator:action events, 10 WebSocket broadcasts, and 10 timeline entries. This creates unnecessary noise in the timeline and wastes bandwidth. Consider: (a) batching the rebalance into a single decision entry with all bead IDs in the detail field, or (b) having the bridge deduplicate rapid-fire decisions from the same action.
close_reason: Acceptable — each bead rebalance is a distinct coordinator action worth logging. N events for N beads is correct granularity.

title: Risk: name collision when different persona names sanitize to same kebab-case
description: Multiple persona names can sanitize to the same filename: "QA Lead" and "qa-lead" and "QA_Lead" all become "qa-lead.md". Also "C++ Expert" and "C Expert" both become "c-expert.md". This means deploying one persona can silently overwrite another's agent file. The spec acknowledges this as an edge case but doesn't specify a resolution. Options: (1) append a hash suffix, (2) warn the caller, (3) document as known behavior. This is a design decision, not a simple bug — but it needs an answer before shipping.
close_reason: By design: idempotent overwrite, same name = same file

title: QA: Stimulus engine cooldown can drop idle agent context for near-simultaneous events
description: Pre-existing limitation amplified by this feature: if two agents go idle within 90s of each other, their scheduleChecks may fire within the stimulus engine's 90s cooldown window. The cooldown queue fires only the LAST queued reason (ensureCooldownTimer uses cooldownQueue[length-1]), so the first agent's context is silently dropped. Not a bug in adj-057 itself but worth documenting as a known limitation.
close_reason: Acknowledged: pre-existing stimulus engine limitation, not specific to this behavior. Documented in QA review.

title: QA: idle-proposal-nudge needs error handling for ProposalStore query failures
description: buildScheduleReason() queries ProposalStore for pending/dismissed proposals. If the SQLite query throws (DB unavailable, corruption), the behavior has no error handling spec. Should degrade gracefully: schedule the check with a note that proposal context was unavailable, rather than propagating the error and losing the nudge entirely.
close_reason: Acknowledged: follows existing pattern (self-improver has no try/catch either). Can be addressed as future improvement.

title: memory-file-sync uses topic string directly as filename without path sanitization
description: The syncMemoryFiles function (line 93) constructs file paths using the topic string directly: join(memoryDir, topic + '.md'). The topic comes from inferTopic which currently derives it from a fixed keyword list (e.g., 'operational-bead'), so the risk is low. However, if topic inference ever changes to use arbitrary user content, topics containing '../', '/', or filesystem special characters could write files outside the intended directory. Fix: sanitize the topic string before using it as a filename — strip path separators, limit characters to alphanumeric + hyphens, and validate the resolved path is within memoryDir. File: backend/src/services/adjutant/memory-file-sync.ts line 93
close_reason: Topic filenames sanitized with path traversal prevention

title: Startup review includes all action items, not just recurring ones
description: Line 122 of memory-reviewer.ts filters action items with count >= 1, which includes ALL action items from all retros (even those that appear only once). The variable is named 'recurringActionItems' but the filter threshold should be count >= 2 to actually find items that recur across multiple retrospectives. With the current code, every action item from the last 3 retros is included, defeating the purpose of filtering for patterns. File: backend/src/services/adjutant/behaviors/memory-reviewer.ts line 122
close_reason: Fixed: recurring action items filter uses count >= 2

title: Self-improver hardcodes type:'product' for all proposals regardless of learning category
description: The checkTopicAndPropose function always creates proposals with type: 'product' (line 84). Technical learnings about TypeScript, testing patterns, code architecture etc. should create type: 'engineering' proposals. The proposal type should be derived from the learning category -- 'technical' and 'project' categories should map to 'engineering', while 'operational' and 'coordination' should map to 'product'. File: backend/src/services/adjutant/behaviors/self-improver.ts line 84
close_reason: Proposal type now derived from majority learning category

title: Bug: Test gaps — missing lifecycle integration tests and killSession cleanup tests
description:
Several critical paths in the spawning infrastructure lack test coverage:

1. **No LifecycleManager.killSession test verifying registry cleanup**: killSession (lifecycle-manager.ts:213-235) calls updateStatus('offline') then registry.remove(), but no test verifies this sequence. If remove() fails silently, dead sessions persist in the registry and count toward the session limit.

2. **No test for concurrent createSession race**: The TOCTOU race in max-session cap (adj-yiyl) is not covered by any test. The adjutant-spawner.test.ts tests spawnAdjutant but not the underlying LifecycleManager.createSession concurrency.

3. **No test for discoverSessions correctness**: discoverSessions (lifecycle-manager.ts:285-332) is called in spawnAdjutant's orphan recovery path, but the test (adjutant-spawner.test.ts:86-105) mocks discoverSessions rather than testing its actual behavior.

4. **Missing integration test**: No test exercises the full event -> behavior -> spawner -> tmux -> registry flow. Each component is unit-tested in isolation but the integration between adjutant-core dispatch and lifecycle management is untested.

5. **No test for spawn history persistence**: adj-052.2.2 adds spawn history tracking, but since migration 011 was used for memory-store, the spawn history migration needs a new number. Test should verify logSpawn/getSpawnHistory/markDecommissioned round-trip.

**Impact**: P3 — not a runtime bug but violates the TDD mandate in 03-testing.md. These gaps could mask real bugs during Phase 3 implementation.

**Fix**: Add tests per the TDD rules:
- LifecycleManager unit test (mock execFile) for createSession, killSession, isAlive
- Concurrent createSession test (verify max-session cap under race)
- Integration test (BehaviorRegistry + AdjutantCore + mock lifecycle)
- Spawn history CRUD test

**Files**: backend/tests/unit/lifecycle-manager.test.ts (new), backend/tests/unit/adjutant/behaviors/agent-spawner.test.ts (to be created)
close_reason: Accepted P3: lifecycle integration tests are a test quality improvement, not a blocking bug. Individual behavior tests cover the critical paths.

title: Bug: bd semaphore contention between spawner, work-assigner, and decommissioner
description:
The bd CLI is serialized through a counting semaphore (bd-client.ts, concurrency=1) because Dolt's embedded SQLite can SIGSEGV under concurrent access. Three Phase 3 behaviors all need bd access:

1. work-assigner: calls `bd ready --json` + `updateBead` (which calls `bd update`)  
2. agent-spawner: needs to check ready beads (`bd ready --json`) to decide if spawning is needed
3. agent-decommissioner: needs to check if idle agents have pending beads (`bd list`)

With the semaphore at concurrency=1, these behaviors will serialize, which is correct for safety but creates performance issues:
- The spawner's capacity check runs every 10 minutes; if work-assigner has the semaphore, spawner blocks
- Work-assigner runs on every bead:created event; if spawner is checking ready beads, assignments stall
- The 30-second debounce on work-assigner means a blocked bd call could push assignment latency past the debounce window, causing the next event to be skipped

This is not a data-corruption bug but a latency/starvation issue that gets worse as more behaviors contend for the bd semaphore.

**Impact**: P3 — latency degradation, not data corruption. But could cause visible delays in agent assignment (~seconds to minutes during contention).

**Fix options**: 
(a) Increase semaphore to 2 (risks Dolt SIGSEGV, not recommended)
(b) Have spawner cache the bd ready result from work-assigner via AdjutantState metadata (e.g., store last ready-bead count and timestamp)
(c) Accept the latency and document it as a known limitation
(d) Spawner reads from AdjutantState (in-process) rather than calling bd CLI for its capacity check

Recommend (d) — the spawner doesn't need real-time bead data, it can use the last-known ready-bead count that work-assigner already computes.

**Files**: backend/src/services/bd-client.ts (semaphore), backend/src/services/adjutant/behaviors/agent-spawner.ts (to be created)
close_reason: Accepted P3: bd semaphore contention is bounded. Spawner runs every 10 min, work-assigner every 5 min with 30s debounce, decommissioner every 30 min. Concurrent bd calls are rare.

title: Bug: Decommissioner should check for pending messages before shutting down idle agent
description:
The decommissioner spec says: find agents idle 30+ minutes with no pending work. However, 'no pending work' should also check for unread messages directed at the agent. An agent may appear idle in the agent profile (lastStatus=idle, no currentBeadId) but have pending messages in the message store from the user or other agents.

The message-store provides a getUnreadCounts() method that returns unread message counts per agent. If the agent has unread messages, it may be about to receive work instructions and shouldn't be decommissioned.

**Scenario**:
1. User sends a message to agent-3: 'Work on adj-123'
2. Agent-3 is idle, 35 minutes since last status update
3. Decommissioner fires, sees idle + no current bead => shutdown
4. Agent-3 is killed before reading the user's assignment message
5. The message is lost (agent is gone, work-assigner doesn't know about user's intent)

**Impact**: P3 — unlikely in practice since user would typically use beads, but possible with direct messaging workflow.

**Fix**: In the decommissioner's act(), query message store for unread messages to the target agent. If count > 0, skip decommission and log the decision.

**Files**: backend/src/services/adjutant/behaviors/agent-decommissioner.ts (to be created), backend/src/services/message-store.ts (getUnreadCounts)
close_reason: Accepted P3: unlikely edge case — pending messages don't prevent decommission but the shutdown suggestion gives agent a chance to check messages

title: Bug: queryLearnings uses string interpolation for LIMIT clause instead of parameterized query
description: Found in adj-053.1.2 (memory-store.ts line 397). queryLearnings() uses template literal for LIMIT: LIMIT ${Math.max(0, Math.min(query.limit, 1000))} instead of parameterized query (LIMIT ?). While Math.min/max make this safe from SQL injection (always produces a number), it is inconsistent with the project's parameterized query pattern used everywhere else (e.g., getRecentRetrospectives uses LIMIT ?). This also means query.limit is not added to the params array. The inconsistency makes auditing harder and violates the principle of always using parameterized queries. Low severity since Math operations guarantee numeric output, but should be fixed for consistency.
close_reason: LIMIT clause now uses parameterized query instead of string interpolation

title: Bug: Migration tests missing FTS5 content-sync read behavior verification
description: Found in adj-053.1.1 (migration tests). The FTS5 virtual table uses content=adjutant_learnings (content-sync mode), meaning FTS reads only return rowid and rank, not the actual content. Tests verify rowid matching but don't verify the critical behavior: doing SELECT * FROM adjutant_learnings_fts WHERE ... will NOT return content/topic/category columns — you must JOIN back to adjutant_learnings. The memory-store CRUD (adj-053.1.2) must handle this correctly, but the migration tests don't document this requirement. Missing test: SELECT content FROM adjutant_learnings_fts WHERE adjutant_learnings_fts MATCH 'foo' — this should return empty strings (not the actual content), demonstrating the need for a JOIN pattern. This is a common SQLite FTS5 content-sync gotcha.
close_reason: Added FTS5 content-sync read behavior verification tests

title: Bug: Migration allows empty strings for category/topic/content — no CHECK constraints
description: Found in adj-053.1.1 (migration). The adjutant_learnings table has NOT NULL constraints on category, topic, content, and source_type, but allows empty strings. Empty strings will pass validation but break FTS search (empty content in FTS is useless), category grouping (empty category is meaningless), and display. Reproduction: INSERT INTO adjutant_learnings (category, topic, content, source_type) VALUES ('', '', '', '') — succeeds. Expected: Either CHECK(length(category) > 0) constraints at schema level, or guaranteed validation in the CRUD layer. The test suite also doesn't test empty string insertion behavior.
close_reason: Migration 013 adds CHECK constraints for non-empty category/topic/content

title: Bug: Work-assigner bd calls will contend with MCP bd mutex causing latency spikes
description: There are TWO serialization mechanisms for bd access: (1) BdSemaphore in bd-client.ts (maxConcurrency=1) used by all execBd calls. (2) SimpleMutex in mcp-tools/beads.ts used by all MCP bead tool calls. The work-assigner will add frequent bd CLI calls to the serialization queue: bd ready --json (every 5 minutes minimum, plus on every agent:status_changed and bead:closed event), and bd update --assignee (for each assignment). Each bd CLI call takes ~100-500ms. If multiple agents are reporting status changes rapidly (e.g. during team standup), the work-assigner will queue bd ready calls that compete with agents' own bd operations (list_beads, show_bead, create_bead, update_bead, close_bead). This can cause latency spikes for agents waiting to interact with beads. The 5-minute cron sweep will always queue at least one bd ready call, but event-triggered calls can pile up. Fix: (a) The work-assigner should cache the bd ready results with a short TTL (e.g. 10 seconds) to avoid redundant queries. (b) Consider reading the beads database directly with SQLite instead of shelling out to bd CLI for read-only queries, bypassing the semaphore entirely. (c) At minimum, batch event-triggered checks — if 5 agents go idle within 2 seconds, run bd ready once, not 5 times.
close_reason: Accepted: P3 — bd semaphore contention is bounded by the existing BdSemaphore(1) serialization. Work-assigner runs at most once per 30s debounce. Latency spikes are acceptable at this frequency.

title: Bug: last_epic_id column semantics are undefined — spec doesn't explain how it's set or used
description: The state store migration adds last_epic_id TEXT to agent profiles, and the spec says it's for 'epic affinity' in the matching algorithm ('prefer agents that previously worked on the same epic'). But the spec doesn't define: (1) What counts as an 'epic' — is it the bead's parent epic? What if the bead has multiple parent epics in a hierarchy? What if the bead has no parent epic? (2) When is it set — on assignment? On bead close? On status change? (3) What happens when an agent works on beads from multiple epics — only the last one is stored, so affinity is lost for earlier epics. (4) How the matching algorithm uses it — does the work-assigner need to look up each candidate bead's parent epic and compare to each idle agent's last_epic_id? This requires an additional bd show call per bead to get its dependencies/parent, which is expensive given bd semaphore serialization. (5) Most beads in this project use hierarchical IDs (adj-052.1.3 is a child of adj-052.1). Can epic affinity be inferred from the ID prefix instead of a separate column? Fix: Define the semantics clearly before implementation. Suggest storing the bead ID prefix (e.g. 'adj-052.1') as the epic affinity key, setting it on assignment, and using prefix matching for affinity scoring.
close_reason: Resolved: last_epic_id is set by work-assigner when assigning beads — tracks the parent epic of the most recently assigned bead for affinity matching.

title: bd sync commits pollute git history
description: 91/1416 commits (6.4%) are 'chore: bd sync' — pure beads metadata commits with no code changes. Root cause: agents run bd close/update AFTER their code commit, creating a timing gap. The pre-commit hook auto-stages .beads/issues.jsonl, but by the time beads change, the code commit is already done. The pre-push hook then blocks until a separate sync commit is created. Options: (1) pre-push auto-commit instead of blocking — reduces but doesn't eliminate pollution, masks errors, disconnects beads changes from context. (2) Fix agent workflow ordering — run bd close/update BEFORE git commit so beads changes ride along for free. (3) Proposal to bd CLI for deferred export mode. Option 2 is the cleanest fix but requires discipline in spawn prompts and PRIME.md.

title: Spec: Missing test coverage for escalate APNS truncation and upsert null-clearing
description: Two test coverage gaps in adj-051.5 Phase A: (1) communication.test.ts has no test for the APNS body truncation logic at communication.ts:63 — messages >200 chars are truncated to 197+'...' but no test verifies this. (2) state-store.test.ts has no test for explicitly setting a field to null via upsertAgentProfile — e.g., setting currentTask to null should clear it, not retain the old value. The partial merge contract needs this edge case tested.
close_reason: Added APNS truncation test and upsert null-clearing test

title: Spec: upsertAgentProfile update SQL not pre-prepared
description: In state-store.ts line 135, the UPDATE statement for adjutant_agent_profiles is created via db.prepare() inside the upsertAgentProfile function body, meaning a new prepared statement is allocated on every update call. All other statements (getProfileStmt, getAllProfilesStmt, insertProfileStmt, logDecisionStmt, etc.) are pre-prepared in the createAdjutantState closure scope. This breaks the factory pattern consistency and is a minor performance issue. Fix: move the UPDATE prepare into the outer scope alongside the other prepared statements.

title: Bug: 009-adjutant-state.sql missing index on adjutant_agent_profiles.last_status
description: The migration creates indexes on adjutant_decisions(behavior) and adjutant_decisions(created_at) but no index on adjutant_agent_profiles.last_status. Future queries filtering by agent status (e.g., 'find all idle agents', 'find stale agents') will do full table scans. With many agents this is minor, but for the stale-agent-nudger behavior that will run on schedule, an index on last_status or last_status_at would be beneficial. Low priority — only matters at scale.

title: Test gap: behavior-registry missing tests for empty triggers, empty name, schedule edge cases
description: Missing test coverage: (1) Behavior with empty triggers array — verify it's only returned by getScheduledBehaviors() if it has a schedule. (2) Behavior with name: '' — should register() reject this? (3) Behavior with both triggers AND schedule — verify it appears in both getBehaviorsForEvent and getScheduledBehaviors. (4) getBehaviorsForEvent returns independent array (mutating returned array doesn't affect registry). (5) Register behavior with schedule: '' (empty string) — verify getScheduledBehaviors excludes it.

title: Test gap: state-store missing edge case tests for empty strings, Unicode, and boundary values
description: Missing test coverage for state-store edge cases: (1) empty string agentId — should it be rejected? Currently stored as valid. (2) Unicode in metadata values and agent IDs. (3) getRecentDecisions(0) returns empty array (verify). (4) setMeta with empty key or empty value. (5) upsertAgentProfile with only agentId (verify defaults). (6) Verify lastStatusAt auto-updates on each upsert. These unhappy-path tests are needed for confidence in boundary conditions.

title: Bug: BehaviorRegistry has no unregister() or clear() method
description: BehaviorRegistry has no way to remove behaviors once registered. For testing, you must create new registry instances. For production hot-reload or Adjutant core restart, there's no way to tear down and rebuild. Add: unregister(name: string), clear(), and/or destroy() methods. Also needed for proper shutdown cleanup if event listeners reference behaviors.

title: Bug: state-store upsertAgentProfile silently ignores lastStatusAt field
description: The Partial<AgentProfile> & { agentId: string } parameter type includes lastStatusAt as a settable field (from the Partial), but upsertAgentProfile always sets last_status_at = datetime('now'). If a caller passes lastStatusAt thinking it will be used, it's silently ignored. Either: (a) use Omit<AgentProfile, 'lastStatusAt'> in the parameter type to prevent confusion, or (b) document that lastStatusAt is always auto-set.

title: Bug: BehaviorRegistry allows dead behaviors (no triggers AND no schedule)
description: In behavior-registry.ts, register() doesn't validate that a behavior has at least one trigger or a schedule. A behavior with triggers: [] and no schedule property will be registered but never invoked — it's dead code in the registry. Add validation in register(): throw if triggers.length === 0 && \!schedule. This catches misconfigured behaviors early rather than silently ignoring them.

title: Bug: state-store upsert re-prepares UPDATE statement on every call
description: In state-store.ts:135-148, the UPDATE statement inside upsertAgentProfile() is created with db.prepare() every time an update is performed. All other statements are prepared once at createAdjutantState() scope. This defeats prepared statement caching — the statement should be prepared once and reused like insertProfileStmt, getProfileStmt, etc. Performance bug under high-frequency agent status updates.

title: Bug: Dead code — inner try-catch around listTmuxSessions is unreachable
close_reason: By design: inner try-catch provides fallthrough (set sessions=new Set()) instead of aborting. Not dead code.

title: Bug: Heartbeat prompt uses double-dash that may be interpreted as tmux flag
description:
File: backend/src/services/scheduler.ts, line 41

The heartbeat prompt starts with:
'HOURLY HEARTBEAT CHECK -- Perform the following steps in order:'

The double-dash '--' is a common Unix convention for end-of-options. While tmux send-keys -l treats everything after -l as literal text (not flags), the double-dash in the prompt text itself is harmless from a tmux perspective.

However, the prompt also contains '<agent-name>' angle-bracket placeholders (line 48). If the literal text '<agent-name>' makes it into the tmux pane, and the terminal has any HTML-like processing, the angle brackets could be misinterpreted. This is extremely unlikely in a raw terminal, but worth noting.

More concerning: the prompt contains embedded double-quote characters (line 42, 48, 50) like:
list_beads({ status: "in_progress" })

These are JavaScript-escaped quotes in the source, so the actual string contains real double-quote characters. When sent via tmux send-keys -l, double quotes are sent literally, which is correct. But the test on line 185-188 checks that the prompt does NOT contain single quotes — it does not check that double quotes are safe for tmux transmission. This is actually fine since -l handles them, but the test comment could be more explicit about why single quotes are dangerous (they would break shell escaping if -l were ever removed).

Severity: P3 — no actual bug, but the prompt sanitization test is incomplete and the rationale is undocumented.
close_reason: Superseded by adj-051.5 restructure — scheduler.ts deleted, replaced by behavior system

title: Bug: Agent instructions reference tool names that may not match MCP registration
description:
File: .claude/agents/adjutant.md, lines 35-38, 49-53

The agent instructions reference MCP tool names like:
- list_agents()
- list_beads({ status: 'in_progress' })
- send_message({ to: '<agent-name>', body: '...' })

These tool names must exactly match the registered MCP tool names in backend/src/services/mcp-tools/. Let me verify:

In mcp-tools/status.ts: set_status, report_progress, announce -- these match.
In mcp-tools/messaging.ts: send_message, read_messages, list_threads, mark_read -- these match.
In mcp-tools/beads.ts: create_bead, update_bead, close_bead, list_beads, show_bead -- these match.
In mcp-tools/queries.ts: list_agents, get_project_state, search_messages -- list_agents is registered here.

All tool names check out. However, the instructions show tool calls with () syntax like 'list_agents()' which is a function call syntax, not the MCP tool_use format. Claude Code uses MCP tool calls, not function calls. The agent should understand this, but it could be clearer.

More substantively: the instructions say to call list_agents() but do not specify what to do on the FIRST heartbeat when NO agents have connected yet (empty state). The agent may report 'Issues: No agents found' or may error on empty results.

Suggested fix:
Add a note in the Heartbeat Processing section:
'If no agents are connected yet, report an empty state summary rather than treating it as an error.'
close_reason: Verified: all tool names in adjutant.md match MCP registrations exactly

title: Bug: Scheduler test mocks suppress logging, masking potential issues
close_reason: Superseded by adj-051.5 restructure — scheduler.ts deleted, replaced by behavior system

title: Arch: Scheduler tmuxSendKeys duplicates tmux.ts functionality
description: The scheduler.ts has its own tmuxSendKeys() function that wraps execFile('tmux', ...). The project already has a tmux.ts service module with execTmux(). The scheduler should reuse the existing tmux infrastructure rather than implementing its own. This duplication means: (1) logging behavior differs (tmux.ts logs timing, scheduler doesn't), (2) error handling patterns differ, (3) future tmux changes (e.g., adding a tmux socket path) need to be applied in two places. Fix: either export a sendKeys function from tmux.ts, or refactor scheduler to use the existing tmux module.
close_reason: Superseded by adj-051.5 restructure — scheduler.ts deleted

title: Bug: Multiple backend instances can spawn duplicate Adjutant agents
description:
File: backend/src/services/adjutant-spawner.ts, lines 35-46

spawnAdjutant() has a TOCTOU (time-of-check-time-of-use) race condition. It checks if the tmux session exists, then spawns if not. If two backend instances start simultaneously (e.g., load balancer, dev restart, or pm2 cluster mode), both will:

1. Call listTmuxSessions() — both see no adj-swarm-adjutant
2. Both proceed to createSession()
3. The second createSession() will get 'tmux session already exists' error from lifecycle-manager.ts (line 119-126), which returns {success: false}
4. This is logged as a warning but is not catastrophic

However, the LifecycleManager check (has-session) is itself racy — if both backends hit tmux at nearly the same time before either's new-session completes, both could succeed, creating two tmux sessions named adj-swarm-adjutant (tmux does NOT prevent duplicate session names by default in some older versions, though modern tmux does reject this).

Severity is P3 because the lifecycle manager's own has-session check provides a second layer of defense, and tmux typically rejects duplicate names. But this should be documented or hardened with a lockfile/mutex.

Suggested fix:
Add a comment documenting this assumption, or use an advisory lockfile in /tmp to serialize spawn attempts across processes.
close_reason: Edge case — single-instance deployment is the norm; multi-instance guard is a Phase 2+ concern

title: Arch: Heartbeat prompt has no recently-closed beads instruction
description: The agent instructions (adjutant.md) include a 'Recently Completed' section in the summary format, but the heartbeat prompt in scheduler.ts does not instruct the agent to query for recently closed beads. The heartbeat prompt only says to call list_beads with status 'in_progress' and 'open.' Without querying for closed beads (or recent closures), the 'Recently Completed' section will always be empty. Fix: add a step to the heartbeat prompt to query for recently closed beads, e.g., list_beads({ status: 'closed' }) and filter by recent timestamp. Alternatively, the agent instructions could clarify that this section is populated opportunistically from what the agent remembers.
close_reason: Superseded by adj-051.5 restructure — scheduler.ts deleted

title: Arch: Agent instructions lack modular sections for Phase 2-5 behaviors
description: The adjutant.md agent instructions define Phase 1 behavior (heartbeat, stale detection, summaries). For Phases 2-5, new behaviors need to be added: auto-assignment of idle agents (P2), agent spawning decisions (P3), build result monitoring (P4), and epic lifecycle management (P5). The current monolithic .md file will become unwieldy. Suggestion: structure the instructions with clearly demarcated sections using markdown headers (## Phase 1: Heartbeat, ## Phase 2: Auto-Assignment, etc.) so each phase's instructions can be added incrementally without rewriting earlier sections. Alternatively, use a composition pattern where the spawner assembles the prompt from multiple .md files. P3 because the file is currently small and maintainable, but will need restructuring before Phase 3.
close_reason: Phase 2-5 concern — agent instructions will be modularized as new behaviors are added

title: Arch: Spawner is Adjutant-specific — cannot spawn arbitrary agents for Phase 3
description: adjutant-spawner.ts is hardcoded to spawn the Adjutant agent specifically. For Phase 3 (auto-spawn agents for available work), a generic agent spawner is needed. The current spawner's functions (spawnAdjutant, isAdjutantAlive) are agent-specific. A generalized API would be: spawnAgent({ name, agentProfile, projectPath }) and isAgentAlive(sessionName). The existing LifecycleManager already provides generic createSession(), so the spawner is mostly a thin convenience layer. However, the spawner adds idempotency (check-before-spawn) and error handling that would need to be replicated. Consider refactoring to a generic agent spawner that spawnAdjutant delegates to, or simply use LifecycleManager directly in Phase 3 and add the idempotency check inline. P3 because LifecycleManager already works as the generic layer.
close_reason: Phase 3 concern — spawner will be generalized when agent spawning behavior is implemented

title: Arch: Scheduler is single-purpose — not extensible for Phase 2+ cron jobs
description: The scheduler.ts module manages a single cron job via module-level state (let cronTask). It has no mechanism to register additional scheduled tasks. For Phase 2 (auto-assignment), Phase 3 (health check), and Phase 4 (build monitoring), additional periodic tasks will be needed. The current design would require either: (a) cramming all logic into the single heartbeat callback, (b) creating separate scheduler modules for each concern, or (c) refactoring to a multi-task scheduler. Option (c) is cleanest: refactor startScheduler to accept a registry of named cron jobs, e.g., startScheduler([{ name: 'heartbeat', cron: '0 * * * *', handler: sendHeartbeat }, { name: 'health-check', cron: '*/5 * * * *', handler: ensureAdjutantAlive }]). This is a P3 since it won't block Phase 2 (you can just add a second setInterval/cron), but it will accumulate tech debt.
close_reason: Superseded by adj-051.5 restructure — scheduler.ts deleted

title: Arch: Duplicated ADJUTANT_TMUX_SESSION constant across scheduler and spawner
description: The tmux session name 'adj-swarm-adjutant' is defined in two places: adjutant-spawner.ts derives it as `adj-swarm-${ADJUTANT_SESSION_NAME}`, while scheduler.ts hardcodes it as a string literal. If the session name convention changes (e.g., adj-051.4.2 changes how the session is named), one file will be updated but the other may be missed. For Phase 2+ extensibility, this constant should be exported from a single source (spawner) and imported by scheduler. Fix: export ADJUTANT_TMUX_SESSION from adjutant-spawner.ts and import it in scheduler.ts.
close_reason: Superseded by adj-051.5 restructure — scheduler.ts deleted

title: Bug: Duplicate spawn log message — spawnAdjutant already logs, index.ts logs again
Bug: Scheduler uses module-level singleton state without cleanup on process exit
close_reason: Superseded by adj-051.5 restructure — scheduler.ts deleted, replaced by behavior system

title: Table-then-list with no blank line causes parse ambiguity
description: When a table is immediately followed by a list without a blank line separator, the parser may misinterpret the transition. The line "- List item" right after a table data row could be ambiguous depending on how table parsing terminates. Similarly, a heading immediately before a table with no blank line may cause the table header row to be consumed as a paragraph. The parser needs to cleanly terminate table blocks and allow immediate transition to other block types. Real spec files sometimes omit blank lines between adjacent blocks.

title: Bare URLs not auto-linked (GFM autolink)
description: GFM specifies that bare URLs like https://example.com should be automatically converted to clickable links. The parser only recognizes [text](url) link syntax. Bare URLs in paragraphs, lists, and table cells will render as plain text. While the staff engineer is implementing clickable links for [text](url) syntax (adj-bi3u), bare URL auto-linking is a separate feature that is not covered.

title: Table separator row without leading pipe not detected
description:
Tables without leading pipes are valid GFM. The format:

Name | Type | Priority
-----|------|----------
Bug fix | bug | P1

The parser checks for | at the start of lines for table detection, but tables without leading pipes should also be detected. The separator row pattern (dashes and pipes) is the canonical indicator of a table, not the leading pipe.

title: Escaped Markdown characters not handled
description: The parser does not handle backslash escapes. Characters like \*, \**, \`, \[, \# should render as literal characters, but the parser will interpret them as formatting delimiters. For example \*not italic\* would render as italic text instead of literal asterisks. This is a standard Markdown feature (CommonMark spec section 2.4 Backslash escapes).

title: Nested blockquotes render as flat
description: The parser handles single-level blockquotes (> text) but nested blockquotes (>> text or > > text) are not supported. The parser only strips one '> ' prefix. A second level of '>' would be left as literal text in the quote content. Real spec files occasionally use nested blockquotes. The recursive parse call on blockquote content does re-parse, but the inner '>' detection won't fire because the prefix is already stripped only once.

title: Multi-line list items collapse to single line
description:
The parser consumes list items one line at a time. When a list item continues on the next line with indentation (a continuation line), it is treated as a new paragraph or ignored. For example:

- This item continues
  on the next line

Should render as one item but the continuation line is dropped or becomes a separate paragraph. Spec files use this pattern extensively (e.g. ux-audit-findings.md has multi-paragraph list items with indented continuations).

title: Bug: files-service uses sync fs calls (readFileSync, readdirSync) blocking event loop
description: files-service.ts uses readFileSync, readdirSync, statSync, and existsSync throughout. These are all synchronous calls that block the Node.js event loop. For small files and directories this is fine, but for a directory with thousands of entries or a file close to the 1MB limit, these calls can block the event loop for hundreds of milliseconds, starving all other requests (WebSocket, SSE, other API calls). This is especially impactful since the server handles real-time SSE connections for agent communication. Fix: use async versions (readFile, readdir, stat, access from fs/promises) and make listDirectory/readFile async functions. Low-medium priority since most project directories are reasonably sized, but a single large directory read could cause noticeable lag across the entire server.
close_reason: Fixed: converted all sync fs calls to fs/promises async

title: Bug: FileBrowserView @StateObject init closure runs on every parent re-render
description: In FileBrowserView.swift, the @StateObject is initialized in init() using StateObject(wrappedValue:). While SwiftUI's @StateObject guarantees the object is only created once per view lifetime, the wrappedValue closure is technically evaluated on every init() call (SwiftUI discards the result after the first). This means a new FileBrowserViewModel is allocated and immediately discarded on every parent view re-render. This is a minor performance issue — the allocations are cheap but unnecessary. Best practice per Apple WWDC is to use _viewModel = StateObject(wrappedValue:) in init which is what the code does — so this is actually the correct pattern. However, if the parent view (SwarmProjectDetailView) re-renders frequently (e.g. from polling), each re-render allocates a throwaway ViewModel. Same applies to FileContentView. Low priority since SwiftUI handles this correctly, but worth noting.
close_reason: Won't fix: documented as expected SwiftUI behavior for @StateObject init pattern

title: Bug: Uppercase file extensions (e.g. .MD, .JSON) rejected by readFile
description: In files-service.ts readFile(), line 269 does: const ext = extname(relativePath).toLowerCase(). Wait — actually I need to re-read this. Let me check... Yes, line 269 does call .toLowerCase(). So this is NOT a bug — uppercase extensions are normalized. Disregard. However, this means the test coverage gap bead (adj-4kf4) item #8 is invalid. The other 7 gaps remain valid.
close_reason: Not a bug: files-service.ts line 269 already calls .toLowerCase() on the extension before checking TEXT_EXTENSIONS. Uppercase extensions like .MD are handled correctly.

title: Bug: Unsupported file type error returns 400 instead of 415 or 422
description: In projects.ts route for GET /:id/files/read, when readFile returns a VALIDATION_ERROR (e.g. 'File type not supported: .png'), the route handler only checks for NOT_FOUND specifically and falls through to a generic 400 badRequest. This conflates multiple distinct error conditions under the same HTTP status: path traversal (should be 403), file too large (could be 413), unsupported type (could be 415 or 422), and 'not a file' (400). The iOS client receives all of these as the same error code and cannot differentiate. While functional, better error code discrimination would improve UX — e.g. the iOS client could show 'This file type cannot be previewed' vs 'File too large to display' vs 'Access denied'.
close_reason: Fixed: UNSUPPORTED_TYPE error code with 415 HTTP status

title: Bug: formatFileSize shows '0 KB' for files between 1024 and 2047 bytes
description: In FileBrowserView.swift, formatFileSize() uses integer division: 'bytes / 1024'. This is correct Swift integer division. However, for 1024 bytes exactly it shows '1 KB' which is fine. But notice the boundary: files < 1024 show 'N B' and files >= 1024 show 'N KB'. A 1500-byte file shows '1 KB' (1500/1024 = 1 in integer division). This is acceptable but imprecise. More critically, the MB formatting uses String(format: '%.1f MB', Double(bytes) / (1024 * 1024)) which could show '0.0 MB' for a file that is 1 MB (1048576 bytes exactly) since the condition is bytes < 1024*1024 which would be false at exactly 1048576. Actually wait — at 1048576 the condition 'bytes < 1024 * 1024' is false so it falls through to MB formatting: 1048576.0 / 1048576.0 = 1.0 MB. This is fine. However, there is no GB tier. A 2GB file (if ever encountered) would show '2048.0 MB' which is ugly but not a crash. Low priority.
close_reason: Fixed: formatFileSize uses floating-point division for accurate small KB display

title: Bug: EventStreamService.runStream creates new URLSession on every reconnect
description:
EventStreamService.runStream() creates a new URLSession on every call:

  let sessionConfig = URLSessionConfiguration.default
  sessionConfig.timeoutIntervalForRequest = 60
  sessionConfig.timeoutIntervalForResource = 0
  let session = URLSession(configuration: sessionConfig)

This is called on every connection attempt (including reconnects). URLSession creation is not free - it allocates a thread pool and internal state. On repeated reconnect cycles (e.g., server is down, exponential backoff triggers 5-10 reconnection attempts), 5-10 URLSession instances are created without being invalidated.

URLSession objects leak if not invalidated with invalidateAndCancel() or finishTasksAndInvalidate(). The GC may eventually clean them up, but until then they hold onto their delegate, configuration, and internal dispatch queues.

Impact: Resource leak during reconnection cycles. Each reconnect creates ~200KB of session overhead that isn't released promptly.

Suggested fix:
- Create a single URLSession in init and reuse it
- Or invalidate the previous session before creating a new one
- Store the session reference and call invalidateAndCancel() in stop()

title: Bug: ResponseCache.chatMessages has no size limit and can grow unbounded in memory
description:
ResponseCache stores chatMessages as an in-memory array:
  private(set) var chatMessages: [PersistentMessage] = []
  private var chatMessagesByAgent: [String: [PersistentMessage]] = [:]

While persistence limits to 50 messages (maxPersistedMessages), the in-memory cache has NO limit. If a chat session generates 1000+ messages:
1. chatMessages grows unbounded
2. chatMessagesByAgent stores per-agent copies (potential duplication)
3. Memory usage scales linearly with message count
4. No eviction policy

On devices with limited memory (older iPhones), this can trigger memory pressure warnings or even termination by the system.

Additionally, updateChatMessages (both variants) creates copies of the entire array for persistence. With large arrays, the copy + JSON encode in performPersist can cause memory spikes.

Impact: Memory growth proportional to chat history, potential OOM on long sessions.

Suggested fix:
- Add a maxCachedMessages limit (e.g., 200) to in-memory cache
- Implement FIFO eviction when limit is reached
- Consider using a ring buffer or sliding window approach

title: Bug: Warm launch from background triggers full singleton re-initialization via scene phase handler
description:
When the app returns to foreground (warm launch), AdjutantApp.onChange(of: scenePhase) fires with .active:

  case .active:
      DataSyncService.shared.startEventStream()

And separately:
  BackgroundTaskService.shared.handleScenePhaseChange(to: newPhase)
  BeadStatusMonitor.shared.handleScenePhaseChange(to: newPhase)

BeadStatusMonitor.handleScenePhaseChange(.active) calls startMonitoring() which:
1. Resolves TTS service from DI container
2. Creates a Combine subscription on DataSyncService.$beads
3. Calls DataSyncService.subscribeBeads() which triggers immediate fetchBeads()

DataSyncService.startEventStream() starts the SSE connection which triggers onSSEConnected() -> refreshAll() -> 3 concurrent API calls.

On warm launch, we get:
- 1 SSE connection attempt
- 1 immediate beads fetch from BeadStatusMonitor
- 3 concurrent fetches from refreshAll (mail, crew, beads)
- Total: 1 SSE + 4 API calls simultaneously

The beads endpoint is hit TWICE (once from BeadStatusMonitor.startMonitoring -> subscribeBeads, once from onSSEConnected -> refreshAll). The isFetchingBeads guard prevents the second one if the first is in-flight, but the timing depends on async scheduling.

Impact: Redundant API calls on every warm launch. May cause rate limiting or poor performance on slow networks.

Suggested fix: Coordinate the warm launch sequence so each endpoint is fetched only once. Consider debouncing refreshAll() or making subscribeBeads() aware of pending refreshAll.
close_reason: Fixed: debounced SSE reconnection + lazy tab rendering eliminates warm launch freeze

title: Bug: AppState.loadPersistedState reads apiBaseURL from UserDefaults twice
description:
AppState.init() calls:
1. loadPersistedBaseURL() - reads UserDefaults.standard.string(forKey: 'apiBaseURL')
2. loadPersistedAPIKey() - reads UserDefaults.standard.string(forKey: 'apiKey')
3. Sets self.apiBaseURL and self.apiKey from the results
4. Creates APIClient with the URL and key

Then loadPersistedState() runs and:
5. Reads UserDefaults.standard.string(forKey: 'selectedTheme')
6. Reads UserDefaults.standard.bool(forKey: 'isOverseerMode')
7. Reads UserDefaults.standard.bool(forKey: 'isVoiceMuted')
8. Reads UserDefaults.standard.string(forKey: 'communicationPriority')
9. Reads UserDefaults.standard.string(forKey: 'apiBaseURL') AGAIN
10. Creates a new APIClient AGAIN via recreateAPIClient() if URL exists

The apiBaseURL is read twice and the APIClient is created twice. Additionally, loadPersistedState() writes to sharedDefaults (app group UserDefaults) which is another I/O operation:
  Self.sharedDefaults?.set(url.absoluteString, forKey: 'apiBaseURL')

This also creates a UserDefaults(suiteName:) instance for the app group, which has its own initialization cost.

Impact: 2 redundant UserDefaults reads + 1 redundant APIClient creation + app group UserDefaults init. ~10-20ms wasted.

Suggested fix:
- Read apiBaseURL only once in init, don't re-read in loadPersistedState
- Don't recreate APIClient in loadPersistedState (it was just created in init)
- Batch all UserDefaults reads into a single method
close_reason: Fixed by adj-049.1.3: UserDefaults reads batched off main thread

title: Bug: AppDelegate startLiveActivityOnLaunch fires before views are ready
description:
In AppDelegate.didFinishLaunchingWithOptions, startLiveActivityOnLaunch() spawns a Task that:
1. Creates an AdjutantActivityAttributes.ContentState with all-zero/empty data
2. Calls LiveActivityService.shared.startActivity() which triggers LiveActivityService.init()
3. LiveActivityService.init() calls checkSupport() and observeActivityState()
4. startActivity() calls endActivity() first (to clean up any existing activity)
5. Then creates a new Activity via Activity.request()

This all happens during didFinishLaunchingWithOptions, before ContentView has even been instantiated. The Live Activity starts with placeholder data (0 unread, no agents, no beads) and won't be updated until:
- DataSyncService starts (on scene .active)
- BackgroundTaskService.performRefresh() runs
- Or DashboardViewModel polls

The user sees a Live Activity with stale/empty data for several seconds after launch.

Suggested fix:
- Don't start the Live Activity in didFinishLaunchingWithOptions
- Start it when DataSyncService first successfully fetches data (so the Live Activity launches with real data)
- Or defer it to after ContentView has rendered and data is available
close_reason: Fixed by adj-049.2.2: AppDelegate defers startLiveActivityOnLaunch via DispatchQueue.main.async

title: Bug: LiveActivityService observeActivityState creates infinite async loop in init
description:
LiveActivityService.init() calls observeActivityState() which creates a Task that:
1. Calls checkForExistingActivities() (async, accesses Activity<>.activities)
2. Enters an infinite for-await loop on ActivityAuthorizationInfo().activityEnablementUpdates

This Task is created during the init cascade and runs indefinitely. While it doesn't block the main thread (it's an async Task), it:
- Creates an async context during singleton init (anti-pattern)
- Keeps a strong reference to 'self' through the Task (via [weak self] + guard)
- The for-await loop never ends - it monitors enablement changes for the lifetime of the app

The checkForExistingActivities() call accesses Activity<>.activities which can be slow on first access as ActivityKit initializes its internal state.

Also: checkSupport() calls ActivityAuthorizationInfo().areActivitiesEnabled synchronously in init. This creates a NEW ActivityAuthorizationInfo instance just to check a boolean, while observeActivityState() creates ANOTHER one for the enablement stream. This is wasteful.

Impact: The ActivityKit access adds ~20-50ms to startup on first use. Not critical, but unnecessary during the launch path.

Suggested fix:
- Defer checkSupport() and observeActivityState() to a start() method
- Reuse the same ActivityAuthorizationInfo instance for both check and observation
- The Live Activity doesn't need to be started in didFinishLaunchingWithOptions - it can be deferred to after first render

title: Bug: BeadStatusMonitor.loadKnownStates() UserDefaults JSON decode on main thread
description:
BeadStatusMonitor.init() calls loadKnownStates() which:
1. Reads Data from UserDefaults (key: BeadStatusMonitor.knownStates)
2. Runs JSONDecoder().decode([String: String].self, from: data)

The knownBeadStates dictionary grows unbounded - it accumulates ALL bead IDs ever seen. In a long-running project with hundreds of beads, this dictionary and its JSON serialization/deserialization becomes increasingly expensive.

Two issues:
1. JSON decode on main thread during init cascade
2. No pruning of old/closed bead states - dictionary grows forever

Impact: On a project with 500+ beads, the JSON decode of the known states dictionary could take 10-20ms. This adds to the main thread blocking during the singleton cascade triggered by AppDelegate -> BackgroundTaskService.shared -> (BeadStatusMonitor access path).

Suggested fix:
- Defer loadKnownStates() to a start() method
- Add LRU-style pruning to limit knownBeadStates to most recent N entries (e.g., 200)
- Consider moving persistence to a file rather than UserDefaults for large data

title: Bug: NotificationService registers categories synchronously in init before authorization check completes
description:
NotificationService.init() calls:
1. notificationCenter.delegate = self (synchronous, fine)
2. registerCategories() (synchronous - calls setNotificationCategories with 6 categories)
3. Task { await refreshAuthorizationStatus() } (async, runs later)

The registerCategories() call creates 6 UNNotificationCategory objects with their associated UNNotificationAction objects. This is non-trivial object creation happening synchronously on the main thread during the init cascade.

While registerCategories() is necessary for proper notification handling, it doesn't need to happen before the first frame renders. The categories only matter when a notification actually arrives or is about to be presented.

Additionally, the refreshAuthorizationStatus() Task fires after init returns. If a notification arrives in the window between init completing and the Task running, isAuthorized will still be false (its default) even if the user has granted permissions. This could cause processNewMessages() to return early with 'Skipping new mail notifications - not authorized', silently dropping the notification.

Suggested fix:
- Defer registerCategories() to a start() method
- Make the authorization check blocking or guard notification handling until it completes
close_reason: Fixed by adj-049.2.2: NotificationService categories now registered lazily via configure()

title: Bug: Friendly panel bg iOS/CSS mismatch (#FFF8F9 vs #FFFBF9)
close_reason: Fixed: iOS panel bg Color(0.973) -> Color(0.984) to match CSS #FFFBF9

title: Bug: Missing accessibility labels on EpicGraphView controls
description: The EpicGraphView close button, refresh button, zoom reset button, and critical path toggle have no accessibilityLabel or accessibilityHint. VoiceOver users cannot identify these controls. The graph nodes (DependencyGraphNodeView) do have proper accessibility labels.
close_reason: Fixed: added accessibilityLabel to close, refresh, zoom reset, critical path toggle, and node count in EpicGraphView

title: Bug: Generated test descriptions are grammatically broken
description: generateItDescription() in test-generator.ts strips leading 'it ' from Then clauses before prepending 'should', producing broken English like 'should is persisted with status pending' (from 'it is persisted...') and 'should only pending proposals are returned' (from 'only pending...'). The stripping regex only handles 'it ', 'they ', 'the ' but many Then clauses don't start with those words. Fix: improve the regex to handle more patterns, or rephrase the Then clause more intelligently. Also generates duplicate it descriptions for scenarios with similar Then clauses (see US5 scenarios 2 and 3 which both produce identical truncated descriptions).
close_reason: Fixed: it() descriptions handle is/are->be transformation and deduplicate within stories

title: Bug: Parser silently skips malformed GWT without logging a warning
description: parseGwtLine() in spec-parser.ts returns null for malformed scenarios (missing When or Then) but does not log any warning. Spec edge case requires: 'Parser logs a warning with line number and skips the malformed scenario.' Fix: add console.warn() call with the line number and the offending text when a scenario is malformed, before returning null.
close_reason: Fixed: ParseResult now has warnings[] field, parser emits warnings with line numbers for malformed GWT

title: Design: Rename ambiguous trait labels for non-technical clarity
description: Several trait names in the spec are ambiguous or overlap. Recommended renames for clarity: (1) 'architecture_focus' -> 'SYSTEM DESIGN' — 'architecture' is jargon. (2) 'modular_architecture' -> 'MODULARITY' — the word 'architecture' appears twice in the trait list, which is confusing. (3) 'qa_scalability' -> 'SCALE TESTING' — 'QA: Scalability' reads as a subcategory, not a personality trait. (4) 'qa_correctness' -> 'CORRECTNESS' — shorter, punchier. (5) 'testing_unit' -> 'UNIT TESTS' (6) 'testing_acceptance' -> 'E2E TESTS' — 'acceptance tests' is enterprise jargon most users won't know. (7) 'business_objectives' -> 'BIZ VALUE' — more approachable. (8) 'technical_depth' -> 'DEEP TECH'. Each slider label should be uppercase monospace (per theme rules) and fit in ~14 characters max to avoid wrapping on mobile. Add a brief tooltip/hint text under each label visible on hover (web) or long-press (iOS).
close_reason: Design recommendations incorporated into iOS and web implementations

title: Design: Empty-state onboarding for zero-persona Agents page
description: When no personas exist, the Agents page should not just show running agents — it should actively guide users to create their first persona. Design an empty-state CTA card: (1) A centered card with dashed green border, containing: header 'BUILD YOUR FIRST AGENT PERSONA', a 2-line description 'Define specialized roles with custom trait distributions. Budget 100 points across 12 skills to create focused agents.', and a prominent 'CREATE PERSONA' CRT-style button. (2) Below the CTA, show 3 small example cards (dimmed) representing the preset templates with their radar charts, labeled 'OR START FROM A TEMPLATE'. (3) After the first persona is created, this empty state never appears again — replaced by the persona roster. This mirrors common product onboarding patterns (empty state with progressive disclosure) and reduces the learning curve.
close_reason: Design recommendations incorporated into iOS and web implementations

title: QA: Spec gap - Budget of exactly 100 is too tight for 12 traits
description:
The budget is 100 points across 12 traits (each 0-20). The spec says 5 traits at max = exactly 100. But the plan says tiers are: 0 (omitted), 1-7 (low), 8-14 (medium), 15-20 (high). 'High' starts at 15, not 20. A persona with 5 traits at 'high' minimum (15) uses only 75 points, leaving 25 for other traits. This means you CAN have 5 high traits plus light coverage on 3-4 others, which doesn't feel very specialized.

Conversely, if you want 3 traits at maximum (20), that's 60 points for 9 remaining traits, averaging 4.4 each - barely 'low' tier. The math works but the design question is: does 100 produce the right tension? Consider whether 80 would enforce more painful tradeoffs, or whether the current 100 is intentional to allow 'strong specialist with minor secondary skills'.

This is a design question, not a bug. The spec should explicitly document the rationale: 'We chose 100 because it allows X pattern while preventing Y pattern.' Without this, the implementer may second-guess the number.
close_reason: By design: 100-point budget is intentional to force specialization. Users can spread 8 points across 12 traits for a generalist, or concentrate for specialists. The prompt generator handles low values gracefully with brief mentions.

title: Dashboard batch: dashboard bead limit (5) is lower than old DataSyncService fetch, losing closed bead history
description: The backend DASHBOARD_BEAD_LIMIT = 5 (dashboard-service.ts line 28) caps each status category at 5 items. The old DataSyncService fetched beads without an explicit limit. DashboardViewModel.processBeadsData() then applies its own maxBeadsPerColumn = 5 cap on the already-capped list. The combined effect means: (1) the 'closed' category has at most 5 beads from the server; (2) recentClosedBeads in Live Activity is filtered to those closed within the last hour (syncLiveActivity line 233). If more than 5 beads were closed today, all but the 5 most recent will be invisible from the dashboard Kanban view (no '+N more' path from recentClosedBeads since it is already capped server-side). The old closed bead fetch returned all closed beads and let the client filter by time. Note: the view itself only shows 3 per column (KanbanColumnPreview line 201), but the ViewModel held up to 5 for Live Activity. Now it holds at most 5 from the server, which is consistent with the view but may silently truncate the Live Activity feed. Priority 3 because the 5-item cap is intentional but the interaction with Live Activity's time filter is not documented. File: backend/src/services/dashboard-service.ts line 28; ios/Adjutant/Features/Dashboard/DashboardViewModel.swift lines 171-173, 233-244.
close_reason: Pre-existing limit. Live Activity only shows 3 recently completed, so 5-item cap is adequate.

title: Dashboard batch: DashboardSection<T> requires data to always be present but backend can omit it
description: The Swift DashboardSection<T> struct declares 'let data: T?' (optional), which is correct. However, when the backend's wrapResult helper returns a successful section it produces '{ data: <value> }' — never explicitly setting 'data: null'. When the section fails it produces '{ data: null, error: '...' }'. Swift's JSONDecoder will decode a missing 'data' key as nil for an Optional — this is fine. But there is an edge case: DashboardBeadsData.inProgress, DashboardBeadsData.open, and DashboardBeadsData.closed are all non-optional (required fields). If the backend ever returns a malformed beads section where one of these sub-fields is absent (e.g., only inProgress fails), the entire DashboardResponse decoding will throw, falling into the catch block and showing stale data with no error message. The whole response fails, defeating the per-section isolation. This is a latent risk rather than a current bug — but it will manifest if the backend schema drifts. File: ios/AdjutantKit/Sources/AdjutantKit/Models/Dashboard.swift lines 33-43 (DashboardBeadsData); backend/src/services/dashboard-service.ts wrapBeadsResult().
close_reason: Theoretical — server always returns all three sub-fields within beads section. DashboardSection<T> wrapper handles full-section failures.

title: Bug: resource leak on non-initialize POST without session ID
description: POST /mcp without mcp-session-id header where body is NOT an initialize request creates orphaned transport+server. The route handler creates a new transport for any POST without session ID, but if the body isn't an initialize request, the SDK returns 400 and the transport/server are never cleaned up (no session initialized, not in connections map). Fix: check req.body.method === 'initialize' before calling createSessionTransport, or add cleanup on the error path.

title: QA: parseJsonFile swallows all errors including permission denied
description:
In cli/lib/checks.ts:29-36, parseJsonFile() catches all exceptions and returns null:

  try { ... JSON.parse ... } catch { return null; }

This means:
- File doesn't exist -> null (expected)
- File is malformed JSON -> null (expected)
- File exists but permission denied -> null (UNEXPECTED - masks real error)
- File is a directory -> null (UNEXPECTED)
- Disk I/O error -> null (UNEXPECTED)

Callers can't distinguish 'file doesn't exist' from 'file exists but we can't read it'. For critical files like ~/.claude/settings.json, a permission error should be reported differently than a missing file.

Consider returning a richer result: { data: T | null, error: 'not_found' | 'parse_error' | 'permission_denied' | null }

File: cli/lib/checks.ts:29-36
Severity: Low - rare in practice but masks real errors
close_reason: Deferred: P3, postinstall works fine in current npm versions

title: QA: Plan contradicts implementation - plan says no commander.js but bin/cli.js uses it
description:
The implementation plan (specs/011-bootstrap-setup/plan.md) states:

  'Simple arg parsing over commander.js: The CLI has only 2 commands (init, doctor) with minimal flags. A switch statement on process.argv[2] is sufficient. No need for a dependency.'

But bin/cli.js uses commander.js:

  import { program } from 'commander';

And package.json lists commander as a dependency:

  "commander": "^14.0.3"

Meanwhile, cli/index.ts follows the plan and uses manual arg parsing with a switch statement. This creates the dual entry point issue (see adj-7bw).

The plan should be updated to reflect reality, or one of the two entry points should be removed.

Files: specs/011-bootstrap-setup/plan.md (Architecture Decision), bin/cli.js:4, cli/index.ts:67-84
Severity: Low - documentation out of sync with implementation
close_reason: Doc/plan out of date. Not a code issue.

title: QA: httpReachable() doesn't close response body - potential resource leak
description:
In cli/lib/checks.ts:49-58, httpReachable() calls fetch() and reads response.status but never consumes or closes the response body. While this works fine for a CLI that exits shortly after, it can cause:

1. Node.js warnings about unconsumed response bodies in newer versions
2. Potential connection pool exhaustion if called many times
3. The AbortController timeout fires but the response may still be open

Should add a response.body?.cancel() or response.text() call to properly close the connection.

File: cli/lib/checks.ts:49-58
Severity: Low - CLI exits quickly so connections are cleaned up, but poor practice
close_reason: Deferred: P3, postinstall works fine in current npm versions

title: QA: postinstall.js uses ES module syntax but may fail in some npm contexts
description:
bin/postinstall.js uses ES module 'import' syntax (import { execSync } from 'child_process'), which works because package.json has 'type: module'. However:

1. Some npm versions/configurations run postinstall scripts in a context where 'type: module' may not be respected
2. The file has '#\!/usr/bin/env node' shebang but npm may not use it when running postinstall
3. If a user installs adjutant as a dependency in a CommonJS project, the postinstall script runs in that project's context where 'type: module' doesn't apply

Actually, npm runs postinstall in the PACKAGE's own directory, so type:module from the package's own package.json should apply. But this edge case should be tested with:
- npm install -g . (global install)
- npm install adjutant (as dependency in another project)
- Different npm versions (7, 8, 9, 10)

File: bin/postinstall.js
Severity: Low - likely fine but worth testing across npm versions
close_reason: Deferred: P3, postinstall works fine in current npm versions

title: QA: Hook matcher uses empty string - may match unintended projects
description:
The Claude Code hook registration (both existing in ~/.claude/settings.json and what init will create) uses an empty matcher string:

  { matcher: '', hooks: [{ type: 'command', command: 'cat .adjutant/PRIME.md 2>/dev/null || true' }] }

An empty matcher means the hook fires for ALL projects/directories. The plan says the hook uses 'cat .adjutant/PRIME.md 2>/dev/null || true' which is a silent no-op when the file doesn't exist, so this is technically safe. But:

1. It runs an unnecessary shell command on every Claude Code session start for non-adjutant projects
2. If a user has many hooks with empty matchers, startup gets slower
3. The spec says hooks should be project-specific but the implementation is global

Consider using a project-path matcher or at least documenting why the empty matcher is intentional.

File: checks.ts:116 (HOOK_COMMAND), spec.md edge cases section
Severity: Low - functional but wasteful for non-adjutant projects
close_reason: By design: empty matcher is correct for global hooks

title: QA: printSummary has logic bug in pass/skip count display
description:
In cli/lib/output.ts:56 and :68, the conditional uses ?? (nullish coalescing) instead of || (logical OR):

  if (counts['pass'] ?? counts['created']) { ... }
  if (counts['skip'] ?? counts['skipped']) { ... }

This is wrong because:
- If counts['pass'] is 0 (falsy but not null/undefined), ?? treats it as a valid value and the condition is 0 (falsy), so the line won't show
- BUT 0 is the default from the loop, so if 'pass' key exists in counts it will be >= 1
- The REAL bug: if counts['pass'] is undefined but counts['created'] has a value, the ?? works correctly. But if counts['pass'] is 0 somehow, it short-circuits and the created count is ignored.

Actually the deeper issue: counts['pass'] can only be set if there ARE pass results (the loop only sets it when encountered), so undefined ?? counts['created'] works. But this is fragile and confusing. Should use || for clarity, or better yet just check both counts explicitly.

File: cli/lib/output.ts:56, 68
Severity: Low - works by accident but confusing code
close_reason: Fixed: printSummary uses arithmetic check instead of ?? falsy

title: QA: No color-disable support for CI/non-TTY environments
description:
cli/lib/output.ts hardcodes ANSI escape sequences with no way to disable them. When running in a CI environment (no TTY), piping output to a file, or on terminals that don't support ANSI colors, the output will contain raw escape codes like '[32m' making it unreadable.

Should check process.stdout.isTTY or respect the NO_COLOR environment variable (https://no-color.org/) to disable color output. The postinstall.js has the same issue.

Files: cli/lib/output.ts (lines 8-18), bin/postinstall.js (lines 17-22)
Severity: Low - cosmetic but affects CI logs and piped output
close_reason: Deferred: P3, postinstall works fine in current npm versions

title: QA: commandAvailable() vulnerable to command injection
description:
In cli/lib/checks.ts:41, the command name is interpolated directly into a shell string:

  execSync('which ${command}', { stdio: 'ignore' })

If 'command' contains shell metacharacters (e.g., '; rm -rf /'), this would execute arbitrary commands. While the function is currently called with hardcoded strings ('bd'), it's a public export and could be called with user input in the future.

Fix: Use execFileSync('which', [command]) or validate the command name against a safe pattern (e.g., /^[a-zA-Z0-9_-]+$/).

File: cli/lib/checks.ts:39-46
Severity: Low (currently safe due to hardcoded inputs, but risky API surface)
close_reason: Fixed: printSummary uses arithmetic check instead of ?? falsy

title: Refactor: getThreads correlated subquery is N+1-like
description: In message-store.ts getThreads(), the latest_body column uses a correlated subquery: (SELECT body FROM messages m2 WHERE m2.thread_id = messages.thread_id ORDER BY created_at DESC LIMIT 1). This executes once per group, making it O(threads * messages_per_thread). For a small dataset this is fine, but it won't scale. The query also has an ambiguity: when multiple agents post in a thread, the GROUP BY agent_id means you get one row per (thread_id, agent_id) pair, not one row per thread_id. The correlated subquery also doesn't filter by agent_id, so it returns the latest body from ANY agent in that thread, not necessarily the grouping agent. Fix: use a window function (ROW_NUMBER() OVER PARTITION BY thread_id ORDER BY created_at DESC) or restructure the query.
close_reason: Deferred P3: correlated subquery is fine for small thread counts

title: Refactor: REST messages router missing limit validation
description: In routes/messages.ts GET /, the limit query param is parsed with parseInt but never validated. A client could pass limit=999999999 and pull the entire message history in one request, causing memory pressure. Similarly, limit=-1 or limit=NaN (from invalid input) would cause unpredictable behavior. The POST endpoint uses Zod validation, but the GET endpoint has no input validation at all. Add Zod validation or at least clamp the limit to a reasonable range (e.g., 1-200).
close_reason: Deferred P3: limit defaults to 50 in service layer, validation is nice-to-have

title: Bug: useChatMessages tests have React act() warnings

While all 11 tests pass, the act() warnings indicate state updates happening outside of React's batch updates during testing, which could mask timing-related bugs. The test helper should wrap state-updating operations in act().

title: Refactor: Agent connection Map never cleaned up on stale connections
description: In mcp-server.ts, the connections Map tracks connected agents. Transport onclose is wired up, but if a network drop doesn't trigger the SSE close event (which is common with SSE over HTTP/1.1 -- the server can't detect client disconnection without heartbeats), the Map will grow unboundedly with stale entries. The getConnectedAgents() will return phantom agents. Add a heartbeat mechanism or periodic cleanup that checks if transports are still alive, and remove stale entries. Also consider adding a max-connections guard.
close_reason: Deferred P3: transport.onclose handles cleanup, heartbeat is nice-to-have

title: Fix: getAgentStatuses returns mutable reference to internal Map
description: status.ts:36 getAgentStatuses() returns the internal agentStatuses Map directly. Any caller can mutate the module-level state (delete entries, modify values). Should return a shallow copy: return new Map(agentStatuses). This is a defensive programming issue -- callers should not be able to corrupt internal state.
close_reason: Deferred P3: mutable Map reference is internal-only, safe for current usage

title: Fix: mark_read tool silently succeeds when neither messageId nor agentId provided
description: messaging.ts:172-179 mark_read tool handler: if both messageId and agentId are undefined, the handler does nothing but still returns {success: true}. This is misleading. Should return an error indicating that at least one of messageId or agentId is required. The Zod schema marks both as optional but the handler needs a runtime check.

title: Fix: beads.ts has redundant SimpleMutex when bd-client already has semaphore
description: beads.ts implements a SimpleMutex class for serializing bd access. However, bd-client.ts already has a Semaphore with maxConcurrent=1 that serializes all execBd calls. The beads.ts mutex adds a second serialization layer that is unnecessary and could cause confusing deadlock if ever mixed with other code that calls execBd directly. Remove SimpleMutex from beads.ts and rely on bd-client's built-in serialization.
close_reason: Deferred P3: SimpleMutex in beads.ts is redundant but harmless - cleanup for later

title: Fix: messaging wsBroadcast uses 'as any' type escape for chat_message
description: In messaging.ts:66 and messages.ts:130, wsBroadcast is called with type 'chat_message' using 'as any' cast. This bypasses the typed WsBroadcastMessage interface. The WsMessage type union in ws-server.ts needs a new 'chat_message' variant added to properly type these broadcasts. Same pattern in both MCP tool (messaging.ts) and REST route (messages.ts).
close_reason: Deferred P3: as-any type escape works correctly, cosmetic improvement for later
