title: [Bug] thumbnailUrl in POST/PUT style routes lacks format:uri validation — accepts arbitrary strings
description:
File: `backend/src/routes/styles.js` (on branch `003-ai-creative-director-foundation`)

The POST and PUT body schemas define `thumbnailUrl` as just `{ type: 'string' }` with no format validation. This means any string is accepted, including empty strings, paths, or malformed URLs.

Compare with other parts of the codebase where URLs are validated, and the response schema where `thumbnail_url` is also unvalidated.

While not a security issue (the URL is stored and returned, not fetched server-side), it could lead to broken images in the iOS app if a malformed URL is stored.

**Fix:** Add `format: 'uri'` or at minimum `minLength: 1` to the `thumbnailUrl` property in both POST and PUT schemas.

title: [Bug] Fastify response schema strips user_id from style preset responses — iOS never receives it
description:
File: `backend/src/routes/styles.js` (on branch `003-ai-creative-director-foundation`)

The `stylePresetSchema` response schema defines `user_id` as a property. However, Fastify's response serialization uses `fast-json-stringify` which by default ONLY includes properties defined in the schema. Since `user_id` IS defined in the schema, this particular field will pass through.

However, there is a related issue: the response schema does NOT include `additionalProperties: false`, which means if the database ever returns additional columns (e.g., from a future migration), they would be silently stripped. This matches the existing pattern in `projects.js` so it's consistent, but worth noting.

No action needed — this is informational. The schema correctly includes user_id.

title: [Bug] PUT /api/styles accepts empty body — updates only updated_at with no meaningful change
description:
File: `backend/src/routes/styles.js` (on branch `003-ai-creative-director-foundation`)

The PUT body schema has no `required` fields and no `minProperties` constraint. Sending `{}` as the body will pass validation and execute an UPDATE that only sets `updated_at` to now, creating a misleading audit trail with no actual change.

The existing `projects.js` PUT handler has the same pattern, but for styles it's more visible since `updated_at` is exposed in the API response and iOS model.

**Fix:** Add `minProperties: 1` to the PUT body schema to require at least one field to update:
```js
body: {
  type: 'object',
  minProperties: 1,
  properties: { ... }
}
```

title: [Bug] Seed file not idempotent for partial deletions — cannot restore individual deleted system presets
Improvement: loadCacheFromDb does not restore contextPercent from SQLite
description:
In backend/src/services/cost-tracker.ts, the CostEntry type has a `contextPercent` field (line 29) that is set in recordCostUpdate (line 257-259), but loadCacheFromDb (lines 930-1002) does not restore it from the database. The agent_costs table does not appear to have a context_percent column, so this value is lost on restart.

This is a pre-existing issue (not introduced by Tier 2), but the agentId restoration code at line 977-981 shows the pattern that contextPercent should follow. Since contextPercent is a live metric that gets re-populated on the next cost_update, this is low priority but worth noting for completeness.
close_reason: All merged to main, build passing

title: Bug: CLI isEntryPoint detection fragile with worktrees and symlinks
description: cli.ts line 232: isEntryPoint uses import.meta.url.endsWith(process.argv[1].replace(...)) which compares a file:// URL suffix to a filesystem path. Fails when: (1) the script path resolves through a symlink, (2) running from a git worktree where import.meta.url path differs from argv[1], (3) Windows paths (though not relevant here). Could use fileURLToPath() for accurate comparison.
close_reason: Edge case only affects worktrees/symlinks. Works correctly in all standard usage. Risk too low to justify complexity.

title: Bug: reporter padRight truncates long feature names, breaking box alignment
description: reporter.ts line 65: padRight(report.featureName, BOX_WIDTH - 22) truncates names longer than 30 chars. This means the header line can be shorter than BOX_WIDTH, while the border is exactly BOX_WIDTH, causing misaligned box drawing. The summary line uses padRight(summaryText, BOX_WIDTH - 3) which also truncates long summaries.

title: Image syntax \![alt](url) renders as broken link
description: Markdown image syntax \![alt text](url) will be parsed by the link parser as a regular link with the text "]" being rejected (or the \! being orphaned text). The [ after \! triggers tryParseLink which will either succeed and render as a text link (wrong) or fail and leave raw syntax visible. Images should either render as an image (if supported) or show a placeholder like [Image: alt text] rather than broken characters. Spec files may include image references.

title: Indented code blocks (4 spaces) not recognized
description: Standard Markdown supports indented code blocks where lines prefixed with 4 spaces or 1 tab are treated as preformatted code. The parser only supports fenced code blocks (triple backticks). Some real Markdown files use indented code blocks. These will be parsed as regular paragraphs, losing their preformatted formatting and potentially triggering inline parsing on code content.

title: Bug: AppDelegate.registerBackgroundTasks called before BGTaskScheduler app launch handler window closes
description:
Per Apple documentation, BGTaskScheduler.register(forTaskWithIdentifier:using:) MUST be called before the end of application(_:didFinishLaunchingWithOptions:). The current code calls it indirectly via BackgroundTaskService.shared.registerBackgroundTasks().

This is correct. However, BackgroundTaskService.shared triggers the singleton init, which calls loadLastRefreshDate() (a UserDefaults read). If this read is slow or if the init cascade from BackgroundTaskService.shared triggers further initialization (it doesn't currently, but adding dependencies later could), there's a risk of exceeding the didFinishLaunchingWithOptions time budget.

More critically: registerBackgroundTasks() is called on the main thread. The BGTaskScheduler.register closures capture [weak self] and create Tasks with @MainActor isolation. If iOS delivers a pending background task immediately after registration (before didFinishLaunchingWithOptions returns), the Task will be queued but cannot execute until the main actor is available. The BGTask might expire before the handler runs.

The expiration handler also uses Task { @MainActor in ... }, meaning expiration handling is also delayed by main actor contention.

Impact: Low probability but could cause background task expiration before handling begins.

Suggested fix: Consider dispatching background task handlers to a background queue instead of @MainActor, and only hop to main actor for specific state updates.

title: Bug: BackgroundTaskService.registerBackgroundTasks uses forced downcast (as!) in handler
description:
BackgroundTaskService.registerBackgroundTasks() registers handlers with forced downcasts:

  BGTaskScheduler.shared.register(...) { [weak self] task in
      Task { @MainActor in
          await self?.handleAppRefresh(task: task as! BGAppRefreshTask)
      }
  }

  BGTaskScheduler.shared.register(...) { [weak self] task in
      Task { @MainActor in
          await self?.handleBackgroundProcessing(task: task as! BGProcessingTask)
      }
  }

If the system passes a different BGTask subclass (unlikely but possible in future iOS versions), this will crash the app with a forced unwrap failure.

Additionally, these handlers spawn Tasks with @MainActor isolation. If the system calls the handler on a background thread (which BGTaskScheduler does), the Task creation itself is fine, but the actual work is deferred to the main actor. If the main thread is busy (e.g., during a synchronous view update), the background task handling is delayed until the main actor is available.

Impact: Potential crash from forced downcast (low probability) + potential delay in background task handling if main thread is busy.

Suggested fix: Use conditional downcast (as?) with error logging instead of forced downcast (as!).

title: Bug: ResponseCache.persistChatMessages debounce task creates retain cycle risk
description:
ResponseCache.persistChatMessages() creates a Task with [weak self] that captures 'messages' by value:

  persistDebounceTask = Task { [weak self] in
      try? await Task.sleep(nanoseconds: ...)
      guard !Task.isCancelled else { return }
      self?.performPersist(messages)
  }

Issues:
1. The 'messages' array is captured by value in the Task closure. If there are many messages (up to 50 PersistentMessage objects), this creates a copy that lives for the debounce duration (5 seconds).
2. If updateChatMessages is called rapidly, each call cancels the previous Task but the cancelled Task's copy of 'messages' isn't immediately deallocated - it waits for the Task.sleep to complete or be cancelled.
3. The persistDebounceTask property holds a strong reference to the Task. If the singleton is accessed during init cascade, this could interact with Swift concurrency's actor isolation in unexpected ways.

Also: The check 'if now.timeIntervalSince(lastPersistTime) >= Self.persistDebounceInterval' allows immediate persistence on the FIRST call but then debounces subsequent calls. This means the first call after 5+ seconds of quiet always persists immediately, but rapid-fire updates during initial data load could skip the first persist window and delay by 5 seconds.

Impact: Memory spikes during rapid message updates + delayed persistence during initial load.

Suggested fix: Use Combine's .debounce operator instead of manual Task-based debouncing to avoid the retain/memory issues.

title: Bug: ContentView creates redundant DependencyContainer instance as @StateObject
description:
ContentView creates:
  @StateObject private var dependencyContainer = DependencyContainer()

This creates a NEW DependencyContainer instance separate from DependencyContainer.shared. The @StateObject means SwiftUI owns its lifecycle. This instance is passed into MainTabView via .environmentObject(dependencyContainer).

However, all actual dependency resolution in the app uses DependencyContainer.shared (e.g., AppState.registerDependencies(), BeadStatusMonitor.init() resolving TTS service, the @Injected property wrapper).

This means:
1. Two DependencyContainer instances exist
2. The one injected into the environment has nothing registered in it
3. Any view that uses @EnvironmentObject to access DependencyContainer gets the empty one
4. All actual dependency resolution uses the .shared singleton directly

This is confusing architecture but not currently broken because no view appears to use @EnvironmentObject for DependencyContainer to resolve dependencies. The MainTabView has @EnvironmentObject DependencyContainer but only for ObservableObject conformance, not for resolving.

Impact: Unnecessary object creation on startup + architectural confusion.

Suggested fix:
- Remove the @StateObject DependencyContainer from ContentView
- Or make ContentView use DependencyContainer.shared
- Audit whether any views actually need DependencyContainer as an environment object

title: Refactor: MCP routes double-disconnect on SSE close
description: In routes/mcp.ts, both transport.onclose (set in mcp-server.ts:135) and req.on('close') (set in mcp.ts:36) call disconnectAgent(). When a client disconnects, BOTH events fire, causing disconnectAgent to be called twice for the same sessionId. The second call logs a warning (MCP disconnect for unknown session) which pollutes logs. While not a bug (the second call is a no-op), it indicates a design issue. Either remove the req.on('close') handler and rely solely on transport.onclose, or add a guard in the route to check if already disconnected.
close_reason: Deferred P4: double-disconnect is harmless, transport cleanup handles it
